I keep reading about the Strait of Hormuz. I keep seeing it in my feed — that it's locked, that it's blocked, that there's a war and oil prices are going up. Truthfully, I know almost nothing about it.

This is the kind of topic I envy people like Richard Heydarian and other foreign-policy folks for — they can connect these dots in their sleep because of their background and training. I don't have that, but I do have curiosity.

So this piece is me trying to understand what's going on and sharing that learning process with you, not pretending to be an expert. SO, WHAT EXACTLY IS THE STRAIT OF HORMUZ?

The Strait of Hormuz is a narrow body of water between Iran on the north and Oman plus the UAE on the south. It connects the Persian Gulf — where many of the world's biggest oil producers sit — to the Gulf of Oman and the Arabian Sea, which then opens to global shipping routes.

At its narrowest point, it is about 21 miles or 33 kilometers wide, but tankers don't get to use all that space.

The actual shipping lanes are like painted lines on the sea: roughly two miles wide going out, two miles wide going in, with a two-mile buffer in between. That means the global economy is funneling giant oil supertankers through a corridor narrower than a Manila expressway.

It's deep enough for the biggest ships on earth, but because the lanes are so constrained and so close to Iran's coast, it's very easy to disrupt. WHY THIS TINY STRIP OF WATER IS THE MOST IMPORTANT ENERGY CHOKEPOINT ON EARTH

Around 20 million barrels of oil pass through the Strait of Hormuz every single day. That is roughly 20 percent of total world oil consumption and about a quarter of all seaborne oil trade.

On top of that, about one-fifth of the world's liquefied natural gas, mostly from Qatar, also moves through this same passage.

Most of that oil doesn't go to the West. Roughly 84 percent of the crude passing through Hormuz is headed for Asian markets — China, India, Japan, South Korea, and the rest of East and Southeast Asia, including the fuel we eventually import.

The United States today imports relatively little oil via Hormuz, but that doesn't protect them from price shocks. Oil is priced globally, so when this chokepoint is in trouble, everyone pays more. WHY FILIPINOS SHOULD CARE

For us, this is not just "Middle East news." The Philippines is a net oil importer. According to MUFG Research, 95 percent of the crude oil that the Philippines imports comes from the Middle East. Even when we buy refined fuel from South Korea, Singapore, or China, a lot of that fuel starts its life as Gulf crude that sailed out through Hormuz.

This is why, even before we fully process the geopolitics, we feel the consequences at the pump.

The Department of Energy has already warned that local fuel prices could rise by anywhere from P2.50 to as high as P10 per liter as companies stagger their price hikes. Government projections say that in a worst-case scenario, inflation could briefly spike into the 6.3 to 7.5 percent range, which hits transport, food, and basically everything that moves by truck.

We have enough oil inventories to get us through the next few weeks, but if the disruption drags on, we'll be forced to pay higher prices or scramble for alternative suppliers. HOW WE GOT HERE IN 2026

On February 28, 2026, the United States and Israel launched coordinated strikes on Iran, targeting nuclear facilities and key military infrastructure. Those strikes killed Iran's supreme leader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei — which is about as escalatory as it gets.

Iran responded with missiles and drones against U.S. bases in the region, Israel, and Gulf states including Saudi Arabia and the UAE.

In that context, the Strait of Hormuz became the obvious pressure point.

The Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps announced that the strait was closed and warned that ships attempting to pass would be attacked. Within days, maritime traffic fell dramatically, with thousands of commercial vessels effectively stuck.

Iran then began laying naval mines in and around the shipping lanes — the kind of weapon that doesn't need to move or "win" a battle to be effective. It just needs to exist and be uncertain.

The U.S. has sunk dozens of Iranian vessels and struck thousands of targets inside Iran, but as long as there is a risk of mines, missiles, or drones, insurers and ship captains will hesitate to send tankers through. WHY IRAN HAS LEVERAGE HERE

Iran does not need to defeat the U.S. Navy to make Hormuz unusable. Its entire strategy is what analysts call anti-access and area denial: make the waters so risky that commercial shipping stops itself.

Mines are cheap, hard to detect, and terrifying when you're steering a ship full of oil. Add coastal missile batteries, drones, and swarms of fast boats, and you get a situation where a single miscalculation can cause a huge spill or explosion.

Geography helps Iran. It controls most of the key islands in and around the strait and sits right on the northern shoreline overlooking the outbound lane. From land, it can track ships, threaten them, or in crises, physically block them.

Historically, Iran has shouted about closing Hormuz but stopped short because it also needs it to export its own oil. The danger now is that with its leadership struck and the regime feeling cornered, Tehran is willing to accept more economic pain for the sake of political leverage. ARE THERE WORKAROUNDS?

There are pipelines, but they are not magic bullets.

Saudi Arabia has an East-West pipeline system with a rated capacity of 5 million barrels per day, running from the eastern oil fields to the Red Sea port of Yanbu. Saudi Aramco CEO Amin Nasser said this week that the company is pushing to bring the pipeline to its full expanded capacity of 7 million barrels per day within days. The UAE has the Habshan-Fujairah pipeline from its onshore fields to the Gulf of Oman, with a capacity of about 1.5 million barrels per day.

Together, at maximum capacity, they can reroute roughly 6.5 to 8.5 million barrels per day. That is still less than half of the normal 20 million barrels per day that pass through Hormuz.

Countries like Iraq, Kuwait, Qatar, and Bahrain have no alternative routes at all — if the strait is effectively closed, their exports are stuck.

One analyst described these pipelines as "shock absorbers, not substitutes," which is a polite way of saying they can soften the blow but not solve the problem. WHAT THIS TELLS US

The Strait of Hormuz is a reminder that geography is still raw power.

A narrow stretch of water, with shipping lanes only a few miles wide, is now influencing inflation rates in Manila, fuel bills in Tokyo, and policy debates in Washington and Beijing.

I'm still learning, and I won't pretend to have the level of analysis that full-time geopolitical scholars bring to this topic, but even a basic understanding already helps connect the dots: when you hear "missiles in the Gulf" or "mines in Hormuz," you're really hearing "brace for higher prices" back home. The Philippine government has arranged two dedicated charter flights to repatriate overseas Filipino workers from the Middle East as the US-Israeli war against Iran continues to threaten civilian safety across the Gulf region.

President Ferdinand Marcos Jr. confirmed the flights during his working visit to the United States. The first will depart from Fujairah in the United Arab Emirates on March 13. The second will fly out of Riyadh, Saudi Arabia, with the earliest departure set for March 14. The Department of Migrant Workers is coordinating both operations.

These are the first government-organized charter flights since the conflict began on February 28. Previously, the government had facilitated the return of 299 Filipinos from Dubai on March 5 through existing commercial routes, but this marks a shift to state-arranged extraction flights — a measure typically reserved for active conflict zones. THE NUMBERS BEHIND THE FLIGHTS

As of the latest count, 1,189 overseas Filipino workers have formally requested repatriation from the Middle East, according to the Overseas Workers Welfare Administration.

The Philippines has approximately 2.1 million nationals living across the Middle East, making the region one of the largest concentrations of Filipino workers outside the country. Of that number, roughly 1.1 million are land-based OFWs under formal labor contracts, according to DMW data. Another estimated 6,400 Filipino seafarers are currently aboard vessels operating in the Persian Gulf, where the Strait of Hormuz remains effectively closed.

The DMW earlier declared the Persian Gulf and surrounding waters a "warlike operations area" and suspended the deployment of newly hired workers to several Middle Eastern countries. Workers already in the region are not being forced to leave, but those who feel unsafe can request government assistance through Philippine embassies and Migrant Workers Offices. THE BAHRAIN INCIDENT

One incident during the current crisis drew a sharp response from the Philippine government. A Migrant Workers Office staff member in Bahrain was accused of being "disrespectful" toward OFWs seeking assistance. Marcos ordered the official recalled and investigated.

DMW Secretary Hans Leo Cacdac confirmed to lawmakers that the employee is being brought home to face a formal probe. The incident surfaced during a Senate hearing on the situation of Filipino workers in the Middle East, where senators pressed the government on the adequacy of its repatriation efforts. THE OFW BUSINESS LOAN PROGRAM

Beyond repatriation, the Department of Trade and Industry announced a new financial program for returning OFWs.

The "OFW Negosyo Fund," administered through the Small Business Corporation, has an initial allocation of P2 billion. Starting March 12, affected workers can apply for business loans through the SBCorp website, mobile application, or designated business centers nationwide.

Trade Secretary Cristina Roque said the fund is designed for workers who face "unexpected job losses" due to the conflict and seek to start businesses in the Philippines.

"By turning remittances into productive investments, we create jobs, stimulate local development, and recognize the significant contributions of our modern-day heroes," the DTI said in a statement. THE BROADER PICTURE

The Philippines is not alone. Across Asia, migrant-dependent economies are monitoring the Middle East conflict with increasing urgency. According to reporting by the South China Morning Post, workers from the Philippines, Pakistan, Nepal, and Bangladesh — who together form roughly 40 percent of the Gulf's labor force — face compounding risks as the war spreads into civilian and energy infrastructure.

Remittances from overseas workers account for nearly 9 percent of the Philippines' gross domestic product, a figure that rises further when indirect economic effects are considered. Any sustained labor disruption in the Gulf would reach well beyond the affected workers themselves, rippling through household incomes, local businesses, and consumer spending in provinces across the archipelago.

The two charter flights this week represent the government's first organized aerial extraction. Whether they remain isolated operations or the start of a larger-scale effort will depend on how the conflict develops — and whether the 1,189 repatriation requests grow in the days ahead. SOURCES

More than 30 governments are now deciding whether to release hundreds of millions of barrels of oil from their emergency stockpiles to prevent the worst energy crisis since the 1970s.

The International Energy Agency convened an extraordinary meeting of its member governments on Tuesday, March 11 — the first such emergency session since the outbreak of the Russia-Ukraine war in 2022 — to determine whether to deploy strategic oil reserves into the global market. IEA Executive Director Fatih Birol issued the call after what he described as "significant and growing risks" to the world's oil supply.

The IEA's member countries collectively hold over 1.2 billion barrels in government-controlled emergency reserves, plus an additional 600 million barrels in industry inventories mandated by regulation. The United States alone holds the largest single stockpile in the Strategic Petroleum Reserve.

According to CNBC, the U.S. believes a coordinated release of 300 million to 400 million barrels — representing 25 to 30 percent of the total emergency reserves — would be an appropriate response. If approved, it would be the largest coordinated release in IEA history. FROM $120 TO $82 IN 24 HOURS

The IEA meeting came at the end of one of the most volatile 24-hour stretches in the history of global oil markets.

On Monday, Brent crude surged to approximately $120 per barrel as Gulf Arab nations cut production amid the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, through which roughly 20 percent of the world's oil supply normally transits. Consulting firm Rapidan Energy described the disruption as the largest in history.

By Tuesday afternoon, Brent had crashed more than 11 percent to around $82 per barrel. Two forces drove the collapse.

First, U.S. President Donald Trump told CBS News that the war in Iran was "very complete, pretty much" and suggested the conflict could end "very soon." Second, U.S. Energy Secretary Chris Wright posted on X that the Navy had "successfully escorted an oil tanker through the Strait of Hormuz," a claim the White House later said it could not confirm. Wright's post was deleted, but not before markets had already reacted.

Saudi Aramco CEO Amin Nasser warned of "catastrophic consequences" for the global oil market if the Strait remains blocked. Kuwait declared force majeure and reduced refinery output, citing Iranian threats against maritime traffic. THE G7 AND IEA MOVED IN SEQUENCE

Before the IEA session, energy ministers from the Group of Seven — the United States, Canada, France, Germany, Italy, Japan, and the United Kingdom — held a virtual meeting on Tuesday to discuss a coordinated response. French Finance Minister Roland Lescure told reporters afterward that "everyone is prepared to take actions to stabilize the market, including the United States."

The G7 ministers requested the IEA provide formal scenarios for a potential reserve release before any final decision. EU Energy Commissioner Dan Jorgensen confirmed the IEA would supply a full analysis of the "advantages and disadvantages" ahead of the member-state vote.

UK Chancellor Rachel Reeves said she used the G7 call to push for "immediate de-escalation" in the Middle East and expressed readiness to support a coordinated release. WHAT THIS MEANS FOR FILIPINOS

The Philippines imports virtually all of its crude oil and petroleum products, making it acutely exposed to global price swings.

Diesel prices are already projected to exceed P80 per liter. The peso fell to a record low of P59.50 against the dollar earlier this week before rebounding to around P58.89 on Tuesday. The Philippine Stock Exchange index lost P672 billion in a single session last week before recovering 2.01 percent.

In Congress, the House Ways and Means Committee approved a bill granting President Ferdinand Marcos Jr. authority to suspend or reduce excise taxes on petroleum products. The Palace has confirmed it will formally request emergency powers from Congress. BSP Governor Eli Remolona Jr. warned last week that a rate hike may be triggered if oil-driven inflation persists.

For the approximately 6,400 Filipino seafarers aboard vessels in the Persian Gulf region, the Strait of Hormuz remains a direct safety concern. Shipping traffic through the waterway remains at 97 percent below normal levels, according to United Nations data cited by Reuters. SOURCES

For the second time since Operation Epic Fury began on February 28, a ballistic missile launched from Iranian territory entered the airspace of a NATO member state.

NATO air defense systems intercepted the missile on Monday, March 10, above the Sahinbey district of Gaziantep in southern Turkey. A US destroyer stationed in the Eastern Mediterranean was responsible for the interception, according to a US official cited by the Wall Street Journal.

Debris from the downed missile fell on empty fields. No casualties were reported.

Turkey's Ministry of National Defence confirmed the incident and issued a warning to all parties, particularly Tehran, to avoid actions that could endanger civilian safety or destabilize the region further. NATO spokesperson Allison Hart confirmed the interception and stated, "NATO remains steadfast in its commitment to defend all Allies against any threat."

Iran did not immediately respond to the Turkish announcement. THE WAR IN ITS 12TH DAY

The US-Israeli war against Iran entered its 12th day on March 11 with no sign of a ceasefire.

On March 10, US Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth warned that Tuesday would be "the most intense day of strikes" in Iran since the operation began. He outlined US objectives: dismantling Iran's missile reserves, neutralizing the Iranian navy, and permanently preventing the country from acquiring nuclear weapons.

White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt said the war would not end until Iran's "complete and unconditional surrender." In the same 24-hour period, President Donald Trump told CBS News, "I think the war is very complete, pretty much." He also said separately that the US will "not relent until the enemy is defeated."

Iran, according to state media, reported over 1,255 deaths and approximately 10,000 injuries from US-Israeli strikes. The Hengaw monitoring group estimated that 4,000 or more members of Iranian military forces had been killed. Seven US soldiers have died.

Iran's Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi told PBS that negotiations with the United States are "no longer on the table" after what he called "a very bitter experience." Deputy Foreign Minister Kazem Gharibabadi said China, Russia, and France have separately reached out to Tehran regarding a potential ceasefire, according to Iranian state television. IRAN IS STRIKING ACROSS THE REGION

Iran launched new rounds of missile and drone attacks against Gulf nations on March 10. Kuwait, Saudi Arabia, the UAE, and Qatar all reported intercepting Iranian projectiles. In Bahrain, an Iranian strike on a residential area killed one person and injured several others, according to reports from the capital.

Qatar's Foreign Minister condemned the attacks as a "flagrant violation" of sovereignty.

Israel continued to face retaliatory strikes, including drones targeting facilities near Haifa, according to Iran's Tasnim news agency. Hezbollah, acting in coordination with Iran, intensified operations in Lebanon. Lebanese health authorities reported that more than 394 people had been killed in Israeli airstrikes on Lebanon over the past week, including 83 children and 42 women, according to the health ministry. Israel said it struck more than 250 Hezbollah targets.

A British warship, HMS Dragon, departed for the Eastern Mediterranean loaded with ammunition and supplies to protect UK military assets in Cyprus, after a drone struck the runway at RAF Akrotiri. FOR THE PHILIPPINES

The conflict continues to ripple through the Philippine economy and the lives of overseas Filipino workers.

A Filipino seafarer remains missing after a tugboat was struck by missiles in the Strait of Hormuz on March 6. The DMW has declared the waters a "warlike operations area" and is monitoring approximately 6,400 Filipino crew members aboard vessels in the region.

Oil prices, which have surged since the start of the conflict, pulled back slightly to around $82 per barrel for Brent crude on March 10, driven partly by Trump's remarks suggesting the war could end soon. The Philippine Stock Exchange index rebounded 2.01 percent after losing P672 billion in the previous session, according to the Daily Tribune. The peso strengthened to around P58.89 from P59.50.

The relief is tentative. Iran's Revolutionary Guard has vowed that it will not allow "one liter of oil out of the region" if US strikes continue, and shipping through the Strait of Hormuz remains at 97 percent below normal levels. SOURCES

The 46-year-old sailor from Capalonga, Camarines Norte had been sending video messages to his family before the attack. Then the updates stopped.

On March 6, the UAE-flagged tugboat Musaffah 2 was dispatched to assist the Malta-flagged container ship Safeen Prestige, which had already been damaged by a missile in waters near the Strait of Hormuz. Security firm Vanguard reported that at least two missiles struck the tugboat during the rescue operation. The vessel caught fire and sank.

The crew included Filipino, Indonesian, and Indian nationals. Three Indonesian crew members are also missing, according to the Indonesian foreign ministry. Four crew members survived. The Royal Navy of Oman is conducting the search and rescue operation, as the incident occurred in waters under Omani jurisdiction.

The Department of Migrant Workers confirmed that it has reached out to the seafarer's family to provide support. DMW Secretary Hans Leo Cacdac said the agency is also monitoring around 6,400 Filipino seafarers currently aboard vessels operating in the region. WARLIKE OPERATIONS AREA

The DMW issued Advisory No. 11 on March 9, officially declaring the Persian Gulf, the Gulf of Oman, and the Strait of Hormuz as "warlike operations areas."

Under the advisory, Filipino seafarers have the legal right to refuse sailing into these zones before a vessel departs. Shipowners are required to implement enhanced security measures. If a crew member is killed or disabled in a warlike operations area, the compensation to the family is doubled.

The declaration came after the Warlike Operations Area Committee of the International Bargaining Forum, the body that negotiates conditions for global seafarers, made its assessment of conditions in the region.

Before the Musaffah 2 incident, attacks on commercial shipping in the Strait of Hormuz had already been escalating. On March 1, an oil tanker was struck near the strait. Two Indian crew members were killed and three others injured when the Skylight was struck by a projectile north of Khasab, Oman, on March 1. The vessel MKD VYOM was struck by a drone boat, and another Indian sailor was killed. THE NUMBERS IN THE STRAIT

Traffic through the Strait of Hormuz has dropped by 97 percent since the US-Israeli war against Iran began on February 28, according to United Nations data cited by Reuters. The strait typically handles more than 153 vessel transits per day. Since March 1, an average of only 13 vessels per day have passed through, according to ship-tracking data from Starboard Maritime Intelligence, analyzed by the Center for Strategic and International Studies.

Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps declared effective control of the strait on March 4. Iran's foreign ministry spokesperson told CNBC on March 10 that "as long as the situation is insecure, all tankers, all maritime navigation must be very careful," and indicated there is no timeline for the strait's reopening.

The International Maritime Organization's Secretary-General Arsenio Dominguez expressed concern over the attacks, calling the situation "unacceptable and unsustainable" and urging all parties to ensure the protection of seafarers and uphold freedom of navigation. 6,400 FILIPINOS ON THE WATER

The Philippines is the largest supplier of seafarers in the world. Thousands work on commercial vessels that transit the Gulf, move oil through the Strait of Hormuz, and service ports across the Arabian Peninsula.

Cacdac assured lawmakers that OFWs who feel unsafe may seek help from Philippine embassies and Migrant Workers Offices, which can coordinate repatriation. Since the crisis began, a total of 399 OFWs and 12 dependents had been repatriated as of March 5, according to the DMW.

One Filipino remains unaccounted for somewhere in the waters off Oman. His family is waiting. SOURCES

Exactly one year ago today, on March 11, 2025, Philippine authorities arrested former President Rodrigo Duterte at the Ninoy Aquino International Airport. He had just landed from Hong Kong. By nightfall, he was on a chartered flight to The Hague.

Twelve months later, he remains in a Dutch detention facility in Scheveningen, and every legal door his defense team has tried to open has been shut.

The International Criminal Court's Pre-Trial Chamber found him fit to stand trial on January 26, 2026. His lawyers tried to appeal that ruling, arguing the chamber ignored clinical evidence of memory impairment. On February 13, the chamber denied the appeal, stating the defense failed to present a viable issue warranting review.

That same day, the ICC authorized 500 additional individuals to participate in the proceedings as victims, bringing the total to 539.

The confirmation of charges hearing ran from February 23 to February 27. Prosecutor Mame Mandiaye Niang laid out the case: three counts of crimes against humanity for murder and attempted murder, tied to 49 specific incidents spanning November 1, 2011 to March 16, 2019. The period covers Duterte's years as Davao City mayor and the first half of his presidency.

Former ICC judge Raul Pangalangan noted that those 49 incidents represent a fraction of the broader campaign. While government records documented over 6,000 drug suspects killed in police operations, human rights organizations have estimated a total death toll as high as 30,000.

Duterte waived his right to attend the hearing. His defense team countered the prosecution's case during the final two days. The Pre-Trial Chamber has 60 days from February 27 to issue its decision on whether to confirm the charges and send the case to trial. THE APPEALS CHAMBER HAS BEEN EQUALLY FIRM

On March 6, 2026, the ICC Appeals Chamber issued a judgment rejecting the defense's appeal against the pre-trial detention review. The ruling confirmed that Duterte will remain detained pending the outcome of proceedings.

This was not the first rejection. In November 2025, the Appeals Chamber denied Duterte's request for interim release, a decision his family said they accepted "with peaceful hearts."

The Duterte family has maintained daily visits whenever possible. Vice President Sara Duterte raised concerns last September about a Philippine Embassy "welfare check" on her father at the detention facility, calling it a "false pretense" visit. The Palace referred the issue to the Department of Foreign Affairs. BACK HOME — THE STREETS ARE ON WATCH

The Philippine National Police is bracing for protests today. PNP chief Police General Jose Melencio Nartatez confirmed that social media monitoring has flagged calls for a motorcade rally along EDSA, ending at Villamor Air Base in Pasay City, the same airfield where Duterte was taken before his transfer flight to The Hague.

Police have increased deployment in Metro Manila and in Davao City, Duterte's political stronghold. Nartatez ordered maximum tolerance.

"We respect your right to protest, but I remind everyone to exercise discipline and comply with authorities' directions to prevent unnecessary conflicts," Nartatez said. WHERE THINGS STAND

Duterte is the first former Asian head of state to be indicted by the ICC. The confirmation decision is expected by late April, based on the 60-day window from the conclusion of hearings on February 27.

If the charges are confirmed, the case moves to a Trial Chamber. If the chamber declines, Duterte could walk free.

The warrant against him was issued as secret on March 7, 2025, reclassified as public on March 11, and he was surrendered to the court by March 12. His initial appearance was on March 14, 2025.

One year in, the ICC process has moved with a speed unusual for the institution, and the defense has not managed to slow it down. The next decision could determine whether the former president faces a full trial, or whether the case unravels before it begins. SOURCES

On Day 12 of the Iran war, Iranian drones breached Emirati air defenses and forced the shutdown of the Ruwais oil refinery in Abu Dhabi — one of the largest refining complexes on earth.

Abu Dhabi National Oil Company shut down the refinery's crude distillation unit, which processes 417,000 barrels per day, and initiated a plantwide safety shutdown, according to Reuters and industry monitor IIR Energy. The entire Ruwais facility has a refining capacity of up to 922,000 barrels per day.

The Abu Dhabi Media Office confirmed the strike: "The competent authorities in the Emirate of Abu Dhabi are dealing with a fire that broke out in one of the facilities within the Ruwais Industrial Complex, resulting from a drone attack, with no injuries recorded so far." THE SCALE OF THE ATTACK

The UAE's Ministry of Defense said the country came under fire from nine ballistic missiles and 35 drones on Tuesday, according to the Washington Examiner.

Eight of the nine missiles were intercepted. One fell into the sea.

The drones were harder to stop. Nine breached Emirati defenses and reached their targets. Whether all nine struck the Ruwais complex or were distributed across multiple sites remains unclear.

Since the conflict began on February 28, the UAE has been one of the heaviest-targeted countries. The UAE Ministry of Defense reported a cumulative total of 262 ballistic missiles, eight cruise missiles, and 1,475 drones launched at the country. All cruise missiles were intercepted. Two ballistic missiles reached targets. Ninety drones breached air defenses. HORMUZ TRAFFIC HAS EFFECTIVELY CEASED

The Strait of Hormuz — the narrow passage between Iran and Oman through which roughly 20 percent of the world's seaborne oil and liquefied natural gas flows daily — has been functionally closed.

Traffic through the strait dropped by 97 percent as of March 7 relative to the February average, according to United Nations data cited by Bloomberg and Reuters.

Maritime intelligence firm Windward recorded only two outbound vessel transits on March 8 — both Iranian-flagged — and zero inbound crossings. International commercial traffic has effectively withdrawn from the waterway.

The consequences downstream are already concrete. Iraqi oil production from southern fields reportedly fell 70 percent to approximately 1.3 million barrels per day, down from roughly 4.3 million barrels per day before the conflict, according to Windward's March 9 intelligence briefing. Iraqi exports dropped to around 800,000 barrels per day, with only two tankers loading and no new arrivals. Storage capacity reached maximum levels, forcing remaining production toward domestic refining. OIL PRICES AND THE VOLATILITY

Crude oil spiked during the conflict. Brent crude reached as high as $119.50 per barrel before pulling back. A brief plunge to around $82 occurred after what appeared to be a mistakenly published and subsequently deleted US government social media post suggesting a naval escort for commercial shipping through Hormuz, according to earlier MCT reporting. Prices have since fluctuated, with the Ruwais strike expected to add upward pressure.

Iran's Foreign Minister stated that diplomatic talks are "no longer on the agenda," according to multiple wire reports. WHAT THIS MEANS FOR THE PHILIPPINES

The Philippines imports virtually all of its crude oil and refined petroleum products. The effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz has already sent domestic fuel prices into their 11th consecutive week of increases.

Diesel price hikes of P17.50 to P24.25 per liter took effect on March 10, with Shell Pilipinas implementing the steepest single increase at P24.25 per liter, staggered over three days. Gasoline prices rose by P7 to P13 per liter.

President Ferdinand Marcos Jr. formally requested emergency powers from Congress on March 9 to reduce excise taxes on petroleum products — a move that had been under consideration for weeks but became a formal request as crude prices sustained levels above $80 per barrel.

Tens of thousands of Filipino overseas workers remain deployed across the Persian Gulf region. The Department of Migrant Workers designated the area as a "warlike operations" zone in early March, triggering enhanced protections and contingency planning for possible repatriation. Filipino seafarers on commercial vessels have increasingly refused Gulf deployments.

The Philippine peso had already touched a record low of P59.50 against the US dollar, and the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas had warned of a potential rate hike if oil-driven inflation continues. SOURCES

A hidden messaging app buried inside a Tetris game. Payments in the five-to-six-figure range, settled on the spot for every piece of information delivered. A Chinese national who flew into the Philippines personally to collect intelligence from three recruited Filipinos working inside the country's own defense establishment.

The Armed Forces of the Philippines has now put a name and a face to the operation.

During a report on GMA's "24 Oras" on Monday, the AFP released a passport image of a People's Republic of China national identified as the handler who ran the three Filipinos arrested earlier this month for espionage.

Rear Admiral Jay Tarriela, the Philippine Coast Guard spokesperson for the West Philippine Sea, confirmed the handler's identity and described how the network operated. HOW THE NETWORK WORKED

According to Tarriela, the Chinese handler traveled to the Philippines to personally receive information from the three recruited Filipinos. In some cases, meetings took place in a third country.

The intelligence was not cheap. Tarriela said payments ranged from five to six digits per piece of information and were "immediately settled" upon receipt.

"We already have the name of that Chinese intelligence personnel who handles this informant system," Tarriela said.

The communication channel was sophisticated. According to The Diplomat, the three used a phone issued to one of the accused that contained a Tetris game with a hidden messaging platform embedded inside it. Those channels have since been shut down.

The recruitment did not happen overnight. Reuters, which spoke to the accused last month under an agreement to conceal their identities, reported that the operation had been running for approximately three years, from 2023 to 2025.

One of the accused told Reuters that a Filipina acquaintance first approached him while he was a junior employee at the Department of National Defense, offering compensation for writing opinion pieces. The arrangement gradually escalated to providing sensitive information about the South China Sea and the defense ministry's interactions with allied nations, including the United States.

He said he did not initially realize he was working for Chinese interests. By the time he grew suspicious, he felt financially dependent and continued. WHAT WAS LEAKED

The three were employees of the Philippine Navy, the Department of National Defense, and a businessman with close ties to a Philippine Coast Guard official.

The information they delivered to the handler included lists of military personnel involved in West Philippine Sea operations and operational details about rotation and resupply missions — the missions that sustain the Philippine presence at contested features like the BRP Sierra Madre on Ayungin Shoal.

The PCG official who unknowingly disclosed confidential data will also face charges, according to Tarriela, though the specific charges have not yet been specified.

The National Security Council had described the case as a "serious national security issue" when it first announced the arrests on March 4. NSC spokesperson Cornelio Valencia characterized the three as junior analysts who had been co-opted "over a period of time" through what he called financial incentives.

All three confessed and are cooperating with authorities. CHINA'S RESPONSE

The Chinese Embassy in Manila denied any involvement.

Spokesperson Ji Lingpeng said China follows a principle of non-interference and "firmly oppose and strongly deplore" the accusations, calling them "malicious smears" and a product of "China threat" rhetoric. THE BIGGER PICTURE

The espionage case is not an isolated incident. The Diplomat reported that the spy operation appeared to be a direct response to the Philippines' "transparency initiative" — a strategy launched in recent years to publicly document Chinese aggression in the West Philippine Sea through video and media releases.

Over the past year, Philippine authorities have arrested more than a dozen Chinese nationals on espionage-related charges. In April 2025, a Chinese individual was arrested operating an IMSI catcher — a device that mimics cell towers to intercept mobile communications — near the offices of the Commission on Elections, just ahead of midterm elections. In February 2025, two other Chinese nationals were arrested for using similar interception equipment near sensitive sites, including the vicinity of the US Embassy in Manila.

The fact that this latest case involved Filipino nationals rather than Chinese operatives points to a shift in tactics — recruiting from inside the institutions rather than sending external agents who are more likely to be detected.

Lawmakers from both the ruling party and opposition have since called for reforms to the country's espionage laws, which currently apply primarily during wartime and do not adequately cover peacetime or cyber espionage. SOURCES

One year after former President Rodrigo Duterte was arrested at Ninoy Aquino International Airport and flown to The Hague, his detention has become the latest fracture line between the Marcos siblings and the administration that shares their surname.

Senator Imee Marcos filed Senate Resolution No. 340 on Monday, March 10 — the eve of the first anniversary of Duterte's arrest on March 11, 2025.

The resolution demands full disclosure of all communications and directives between the Executive Department and the International Criminal Court related to Duterte's arrest and continued detention. WHAT THE RESOLUTION SAYS

Imee Marcos used pointed language. The resolution describes Duterte's arrest as an "extraordinary rendition" and argues that his "unlawful deprivation of liberty constitutes a continuing violation that endures for as long as the restraint persists without valid legal and judicial basis."

The senator accused the government of failing to "provide accountability or meaningful redress for the ongoing impairment of FPRRD's constitutional and statutory rights."

She went further, claiming "conflicting statements" by key government officials "raise serious concerns on their candor and integrity."

And then the broader warning: "The Executive Department's persistent lack of transparency, its grave violations of the Bill of Rights, and its circumvention of the Philippine judicial system now give rise to serious and imminent concerns that similar actions may again be undertaken against the political opponents of the administration and retired police officials — thereby threatening to normalize the erosion of constitutional safeguard and the rule of law." THE PALACE RESPONSE

The reply from the Presidential Communications Office came the same day.

Undersecretary Claire Castro did not hold back. She pointed out that Imee had previously issued a report on Duterte's arrest that the Palace considers "biased" — a report that recommended pursuing legal action against law enforcement officials involved in the turnover.

Castro then questioned the logic of filing a new resolution on the same topic, asking whether the claims in Imee's earlier report were "so flimsy and shallow" that a formal Senate resolution was now needed to advance them.

She pushed back on the framing of the resolution entirely, urging Imee and the Senate to consider the interests of the victims and complainants in the ICC's crimes against humanity case.

"They should be equitable and neutral for all Filipinos. There's a distinction between merely observing and intensely scrutinizing." THE LEGAL BACKDROP

Duterte faces three charges of crimes against humanity before the ICC in connection with his administration's anti-drug campaign.

Government figures place the death toll from that campaign at a minimum of 6,000. Human rights organizations have put the number above 30,000, with victims including children as young as three years old.

The Philippines withdrew from the Rome Statute in 2019, during Duterte's presidency. But the ICC retains jurisdiction over crimes committed while the country was a member state — a period that began on November 1, 2011 and ended with the withdrawal taking effect on March 17, 2019.

Duterte was arrested on March 11, 2025, upon arrival at NAIA Terminal 3 from Hong Kong. He departed for The Hague the same evening. THE FAMILY DIVIDE

What makes this politically unusual is the family dynamic. Imee Marcos is the sister of President Ferdinand Marcos Jr.

The two have found themselves on opposite sides of the Duterte question repeatedly over the past year. The Palace has maintained that it will not intervene in ICC proceedings. Imee has consistently pushed for Duterte's release and questioned the legality of his arrest.

Resolution 340 is the latest escalation in that pattern — a formal Senate instrument filed by the president's own sister, directly challenging the executive branch's handling of a case the president has chosen not to block.

The ICC had previously barred Imee from visiting Duterte in his cell at the Scheveningen detention facility in The Hague. WHAT COMES NEXT

The resolution calls for a Senate inquiry. Whether it gains traction in committee depends on whether other senators sign on.

The Palace has staked everything on fairness to the victims.

Imee has staked everything on constitutional rights.

One year in, neither side appears ready to give ground. SOURCES

The Anne Curtis remark was disgusting. Rep. Bong Suntay used a sexual fantasy about a married woman to argue that VP Sara Duterte's hitman threat was "just imagination." The House struck it from the record. Women's groups condemned it. And then Atty. Ferdinand Topacio doubled down on his radio show, admitting he once felt lustful toward Rep. Sarah Elago, defending Suntay's comments, and blaming Jasmine Curtis for posting bikini photos.

That is a real issue. It deserves the outrage it's getting. Gabriela is right to file complaints. Rep. Elago is right to demand accountability.

But here's what I need us to remember.

Suntay made those comments while defending a specific claim: that VP Sara's recorded, on-camera admission of contracting someone to kill the President, the First Lady, and the Speaker of the House was just a "thought," not a crime.

That recorded threat is still the story. "Sinabi ko sa kanya, kapag pinatay ako, patayin mo si BBM, si Liza Araneta, at si Martin Romualdez. No joke, no joke." She confirmed it to reporters. She called it "revenge from the grave." The NBI issued a subpoena. The PNP admitted they had no concrete lead on who the hitman was.

As of today, nobody has publicly identified that person.

That should terrify us more than a congressman's fantasies.

Meanwhile, two impeachment complaints have been declared sufficient in substance by the House Committee on Justice. VP Sara has been ordered to respond within 10 days. Ramil Madriaga, her alleged former bagman, is willing to testify about confidential fund transfers, about names like "Mary Grace Piattos" and "Kokoy Villamin" appearing in liquidation records for P612 million in OVP and DepEd funds. The Philippine Statistics Authority found no birth, marriage, or death records for some of these names. Madriaga was reportedly offered between P500 million and P1 billion not to testify.

Duterte filed a perjury case against Madriaga. That is her right. But it also tells us something: this testimony worries her enough to fight it in court.

So here's what I'm asking.

Be angry about the Anne Curtis comment. Be angry about Topacio's remarks toward Elago. Both reflect a culture of misogyny that has no place in Congress or public life. And no, calling for focus on the impeachment does not mean we dismiss what happened to Anne Curtis or Sarah Elago. The misogyny is real, it deserves consequences, and those consequences should be pursued on their own terms. Letting it get swallowed by the impeachment news cycle actually protects the men who said it. They get to hide behind a bigger scandal instead of answering for what they did.

The impeachment is the institution. The confidential funds are the receipts. The hitman threat is the crime admitted on camera.

After the outrage fades, after the trending topics move on, who is still asking: who was the hitman? Has that person been identified? What are the legal consequences for a sitting Vice President who said, on record, "no joke, no joke"?

Seven months into the Senate's flood control probe, the two senators at the center of it ended up in a live floor fight over who was investigating — and who was protecting the wrong people.

Senate President Pro Tempore Panfilo "Ping" Lacson took the floor on Tuesday, March 10, delivering a privilege speech directly responding to Sen. Rodante Marcoleta's speech the previous day.

The exchange went past prepared remarks and into open interpellation, ending with the session briefly suspended and the matter referred to the Rules Committee. WHAT MARCOLETA SAID ON MONDAY

Marcoleta delivered his privilege speech on March 9, targeting Lacson's leadership of the Blue Ribbon Committee.

His central charge: seven months after the Senate began its flood control probe, Lacson had still not released a comprehensive committee report — offering only a partial one, and even that document, Marcoleta argued, bore no chairman's signature.

"The people have waited long enough. It has been seven months since the first Blue Ribbon Hearing. Yet the said partial report does not bear the signature of the Chairman," Marcoleta said.

He also questioned why the committee had not summoned 18 individuals claiming to be former Marines, who alleged they had delivered suitcases of cash from former Congressman Zaldy Co to various government officials. LACSON'S RESPONSE

Lacson disputed the unsigned-report claim on procedural grounds.

Soft and hard copies, he said, were sent to all committee members' offices as early as February 11. Signatures come when the report is formally filed and sponsored. Marcoleta's office had the document for a month and had neither signed it nor entered a dissent.

"Why is he now looking for a Blue Ribbon Committee report that had been in his office for a month," Lacson said.

Only Sen. Paolo "Bam" Aquino IV signed the partial draft report.

On the ex-Marines, Lacson said the figures in their allegations — an alleged P805-billion cash delivery scheme — were logistically impossible to execute. The committee's job, he said, is to build cases on verifiable evidence. THE HARDER QUESTIONS

Lacson did not limit his response to the procedural fight.

He raised Marcoleta's record on the West Philippine Sea. In a Commission on Appointments hearing earlier this year, Marcoleta reportedly suggested giving up the Kalayaan Island Group (KIG). He later sought to have that remark deleted from the CA minutes, claiming it was taken out of context.

Lacson also cited Marcoleta's February 2026 statements disputing the role of Tomas Cloma — the Filipino fishing magnate who claimed the Spratly Islands as "Freedomland" in 1956, later ceding the claim to the Philippine government. That cession became the legal basis for Presidential Decree 1596 in 1978, which established the Municipality of Kalayaan.

Marcoleta called Cloma "a seafarer, not a discoverer," and proposed removing his role from official records.

Lacson said Marcoleta's statements on the Spratlys and the KIG had been picked up and republished on China's Ministry of Foreign Affairs website.

He closed with two questions:

"On the West Philippine Sea — which side of the South China Sea is Sen. Marcoleta on? On the anomalous graft-ridden flood control projects — is Sen. Marcoleta for the Discayas or the People of the Philippines?"

"I think we know the answers." THE DISCAYA QUESTION

Curlee (Pacifico) and Sarah (Cezarah) Discaya are the contractor couple whose firms secured over P207 billion in flood control contracts. They testified before the Senate in September 2025 and implicated multiple lawmakers and officials in an alleged kickback scheme.

Lacson raised a specific undisclosed conflict during his speech: a family member of Marcoleta had served as an independent director of insurance companies that provided performance bonds for Discaya-linked contractor firms — a connection Marcoleta had not disclosed to committee members.

Lacson also questioned Marcoleta's persistent push to make the Discayas state witnesses, pointing out that the couple are primary subjects of the contracts under investigation. THE FLOOR EXCHANGE

Marcoleta rose after the speech and accused Lacson of going ad hominem.

"It seemed like it turned into an ad hominem. If I respond to your accusations, the discussion will get sidetracked again," Marcoleta said.

Lacson: "Who started all this? Enough is enough."

Senate Majority Leader Miguel Zubiri backed the decision to let Lacson respond early in the session. He noted that Marcoleta's Monday speech had singled out a fellow senator — who had every right to defend himself.

"That member of the Senate will stand and defend themselves. We have to give them priority," Zubiri said. BACKGROUND

The Senate Blue Ribbon Committee launched its flood control probe in August 2025 following Marcos's disclosure of widespread anomalies in the government's flood mitigation program.

The administration allocated P545.6 billion for flood control projects from July 2022 to May 2025. Fifteen out of more than 2,400 accredited contractors received roughly P100 billion — about 18 percent of the total.

As of November 2025, the Independent Commission for Infrastructure had referred 37 individuals to the Office of the Ombudsman, including lawmakers, officials, and contractors. Zaldy Co, former Ako Bicol party-list representative and House Appropriations Committee chair, left for abroad in August 2025 and has not returned.

Marcoleta chaired the Blue Ribbon Committee at the start of the probe. Lacson took over as chairman when he was elected Senate President Pro Tempore in September 2025. SOURCES

The move from "considering" to "formally requesting" happened Monday.

Palace Press Officer Claire Castro announced that the Department of Energy would submit the official request to Congress on Monday, March 9, asking lawmakers to grant President Ferdinand Marcos Jr. emergency powers to reduce excise taxes on petroleum products.

Marcos was in New York at the time, on a working visit to the United Nations from March 9 to 10. WHAT CHANGED

For weeks, Marcos had said he was weighing the use of emergency powers if oil prices reached or surpassed $80 per barrel. That threshold has long been crossed.

Crude oil surpassed $100 per barrel for the first time since 2022 after the Iran war disrupted shipping and production in the Middle East. Brent crude spiked to $119.50 per barrel before pulling back to around $106. West Texas Intermediate reached $119.48 before settling near $103.

The Strait of Hormuz — through which roughly 20% of the world's seaborne oil travels — has been effectively closed since the conflict escalated in late February.

As of Monday, the Philippines was heading into its 11th straight week of diesel and kerosene price increases. THE NUMBERS THIS WEEK

The Department of Energy announced diesel price hikes of P17.50 to P24.25 per liter starting Tuesday, March 10.

Shell Pilipinas was set to implement the steepest increase at P24.25 per liter, staggered over three days. Petron set its hike at P19.20 per liter. Total at P20.20. Chevron at P17.50. Jetti Petroleum at P19 per liter. Seaoil between P21 and P23 per liter.

The DOE had appealed to oil companies to stagger their increases rather than applying them all at once on Tuesday.

Gasoline prices were also set to rise by P7 to P13 per liter for the same week. POWER RATES NEXT

The fuel price surge is not stopping at the pump.

DOE Secretary Sharon Garin warned on Monday that electricity rates could increase by as much as 16 percent by April if global fuel prices keep rising. She cited higher transportation and insurance costs for coal, as well as disrupted liquefied natural gas supply after Qatar halted gas exports amid regional tensions, forcing buyers to seek more expensive alternatives.

Garin said the DOE is working on mitigation measures for both grid-connected and off-grid areas before the increases fully take effect. THE CONGRESSIONAL TRACK

The House Ways and Means Committee approved House Bill 8292 — filed by Speaker Faustino Dy III and Majority Leader Ferdinand Alexander Marcos — which would allow the President to suspend or reduce fuel excise taxes under two conditions.

First, Dubai crude must have averaged $80 per barrel or more for at least three consecutive months. Second, the President must have declared a national emergency resulting in significant domestic fuel price increases, as certified by the Secretary of Energy.

Any suspension would be capped at six months, extendable only by Congress through a joint resolution, and no longer than a total of one calendar year.

The excise tax on diesel currently stands at P6 per liter. On gasoline, it is P10 per liter.

The DOE's formal request to Congress on Monday is the executive branch's move to push that bill through both chambers. BSP LOSES ROOM TO CUT

The broader economic picture got tighter at the same time.

"With the prospects of oil prices rising and the resultant likely acceleration of inflation, the door shut firmly on the BSP's easing cycle," Nicholas Mapa, chief economist at Metrobank, said in a commentary published Monday.

BSP Governor Eli Remolona had previously warned that the central bank might reverse course and raise interest rates if global oil prices reached $100 per barrel and the US dollar continued rising. Both of those conditions were already in play.

The BSP had cut its key policy rate to 4.25 percent — its lowest level in over three years — following six consecutive reductions since August 2024. Any further rate hike to contain inflation would effectively end that pro-growth cycle. MARCOS IN NEW YORK

While the DOE submitted its request to Congress in Manila, Marcos was at the UN Headquarters in New York, where he met with Secretary-General António Guterres on Monday. The meetings covered the Philippines' bid for a non-permanent seat on the UN Security Council for the 2027-2028 term and the situation in the Middle East, where an estimated 2.5 million Filipinos live or work.

His working visit ran from March 9 to 10.

With oil already above $100 per barrel, the formal request now shifts the weight to Congress — specifically, how fast both chambers can move the bill through plenary and Senate deliberations. SOURCES

Brent crude swung nearly 26 percent in a single day.

On Monday, March 10, the global benchmark spiked to almost $120 per barrel as the war between the United States, Israel, and Iran entered its second week with no resolution in sight. Hours later, it collapsed to $88.36 per barrel, a drop of nearly 11 percent, after U.S. President Donald Trump signaled the conflict could end soon, according to CNBC and the New York Times.

U.S. crude fell more than 10 percent to $85.17 per barrel, according to CNBC.

By Tuesday, Brent had stabilized around $90 to $92 per barrel, according to the New York Times. WHAT TRUMP SAID

Trump issued the threat on Truth Social.

"If Iran does anything that stops the flow of Oil within the Strait of Hormuz, they will be hit by the United States of America TWENTY TIMES HARDER than they have been hit thus far," he wrote, according to Euronews.

In a phone interview with CBS News earlier Monday, Trump said the conflict was "very complete, pretty much" and mentioned he was "thinking about taking it over" in reference to the Strait of Hormuz, according to NBC News.

At a Florida press conference, he said the war would end "very soon" and characterized the operation as "just an excursion into something that had to be done." He also said the U.S. Navy could escort tankers through the strait. Bloomberg reported that Trump plans to waive oil-related sanctions and have the Navy guarantee safe passage. IRAN'S RESPONSE

Iran responded with escalation, not concessions.

On Tuesday, Iran's Revolutionary Guards Corps said they would not allow "one litre of oil" to be shipped from the Middle East if U.S. and Israeli attacks continue, according to Reuters and MarineLink.

"We are the ones who will determine the end of the war," the IRGC spokesperson said, according to state media as reported by Asharq Al-Awsat and Euronews.

An Iranian Foreign Ministry spokesperson warned on Monday that oil tankers navigating the Strait of Hormuz should "be very careful," according to CNBC. No timeline was given for reopening the waterway.

Tanker traffic through the strait has dropped from 37 vessels on February 27 to near zero in the days since military operations began, according to previous MCT reporting based on Reuters data. ARAMCO'S WARNING

Saudi Aramco, the world's largest oil exporter, used its Tuesday earnings call to issue a stark warning.

CEO Amin Nasser said there would be "catastrophic consequences" for global oil markets if the conflict continues to disrupt shipping in the Strait of Hormuz, according to Reuters and MarineLink.

"While we have faced disruptions in the past, this one by far is the biggest crisis the region's oil and gas industry has faced," Nasser said.

He noted that global crude oil inventories are at a five-year low, warning that the disruption will accelerate drawdowns and that restoring shipping in the strait is critical, according to Reuters. G7 DEBATES EMERGENCY RESERVES

G7 finance ministers held a virtual meeting on Monday to assess the impact of the war on energy markets, convening with International Energy Agency Executive Director Fatih Birol, according to Reuters.

The IEA urged a coordinated release of emergency oil reserves, according to Japanese Finance Minister Satsuki Katayama, who spoke at a press briefing after the call, as reported by Reuters.

But the G7 did not act. A G7 official told Reuters there was "broad agreement" not to release strategic oil reserves "just yet." The ministers said they were prepared to take "necessary measures" to stabilize energy supply, including tapping reserves, but chose not to proceed immediately. WHAT THIS MEANS FOR THE PHILIPPINES

The Philippines imports more than 90 percent of its fuel, according to BNC. Every dollar increase per barrel pushes up domestic prices, feeds into transportation costs, and cascades into food and goods.

The House committee on ways and means approved a bill on Tuesday to give President Marcos the power to suspend fuel excise taxes. The Department of Economy, Planning, and Development has warned that if oil reaches $140 and the Strait stays closed, diesel could surge to P96 per liter.

Monday's swing illustrates how fragile the current equilibrium is. One Trump statement cratered prices. One IRGC statement could send them back up. SOURCES

How fast can the Philippine government move when fuel hits P96 per liter?

On Tuesday, March 10, the House committee on ways and means approved an unnumbered substitute bill that would allow President Ferdinand Marcos Jr. to temporarily suspend or reduce excise taxes on fuel products during national emergencies.

The committee vote came one day after the bill was filed. Manila Representative Rolando Valeriano proposed its adoption. Committee chairman Rep. Miro Quimbo of Marikina endorsed the motion after receiving no objections, then approved a second motion to advance the measure to the plenary for immediate action, according to Inquirer.

"We have to fast-track this," Quimbo said on Monday. "We're waiting for the presidential certification so we can actually approve it as soon as possible." WHAT THE BILL DOES

House Bill No. 8292 was filed on Monday by Speaker Faustino "Bojie" Dy III and Majority Leader Ferdinand Alexander "Sandro" Marcos. It proposes an amendment to Section 148 of the National Internal Revenue Code of 1997.

Under the bill, the President may suspend or reduce excise taxes on specific petroleum products based on the recommendation of the Secretaries of Finance and Energy. Two conditions must be met.

First, the average price of Dubai crude oil must reach or exceed $80 per barrel for at least three consecutive months prior to the suspension order.

Second, the President must have declared a national emergency or calamity resulting in significant increases in domestic fuel prices, as certified by the Secretary of Energy.

Any suspension or reduction would last for a maximum of six months, unless Congress extends or terminates it earlier through a joint resolution. The total duration may not exceed one calendar year. Once the conditions are resolved, the original excise tax rates automatically apply, according to Inquirer.

Currently, the excise tax on diesel stands at P6 per liter. Gasoline is taxed at P10 per liter. THE FISCAL COST

The Department of Finance has warned that eliminating the fuel excise tax from May through December 2026 would cost the treasury an estimated P136 billion in lost revenue.

Finance Undersecretary Karlo Fermin Adriano broke the figure down during a committee hearing on Monday: P121.4 billion from lost excise tax collections and P14.6 billion from reduced value-added tax revenues, according to Inquirer.

For every peso removed from the P6 per liter diesel excise tax, the government loses P9 billion to P10 billion. For every peso removed from the P10 per liter gasoline excise tax, the loss is P5 billion to P6 billion.

The DOF, in a separate statement, placed the total foregone revenue from suspending all fuel excise taxes and VAT at P147.1 billion, according to ONE News. TWO SCENARIOS ON THE TABLE

The Department of Economy, Planning, and Development presented two scenarios during Monday's hearing through Undersecretary Rosemarie Edillon.

Under Scenario 1, the Middle East conflict de-escalates and Dubai crude settles at around $80 per barrel. Diesel would drop from P74.22 per liter to P67.50 per liter with the excise tax suspended.

Under Scenario 2, the Strait of Hormuz stays closed and oil spikes to $140 per barrel. Diesel surges to P96 per liter. Suspending the excise tax brings it down to P90.04. Gasoline reaches P82.20 per liter without suspension, dropping to roughly P72 if the tax is removed.

Full-year inflation under Scenario 1 projects between 3.9 and 4.2 percent. Under the worst case, it climbs to between 4.5 and 4.8 percent without intervention, according to Rappler. THE SENATE IS MOVING TOO

The House is not alone. Senate President Vicente Sotto III confirmed that the upper chamber has been in continuous communication with the Office of the Executive Secretary on the fuel excise legislation since the weekend, according to Philstar.

"We will prepare for it. The Senate is committed to providing any assistance possible to the executive branch," Sotto said.

The Senate's ways and means, finance, and energy committees are scheduled to begin hearings on the fuel excise suspension proposals, according to Philstar.

Presidential Undersecretary Claire Castro told Philippine media during Marcos' working visit to New York that the Department of Energy plans to formally request Congress to grant the President special powers to lower the excise tax, according to Philstar. FROM HEARING TO VOTE IN 24 HOURS

What took years of debate during the TRAIN law's original passage in 2017 is now moving through committee in a single day. The difference is context. Oil has been above $80 per barrel for months. Brent crude spiked above $120 per barrel before pulling back. The Strait of Hormuz has been functionally closed since late February.

Monday's hearing saw officials from the DOF and DEPDev lay out the numbers. Tuesday's committee session approved the bill.

The next step is plenary approval in the House, followed by Senate deliberations. Both chambers appear to be operating under the assumption that Marcos will certify the bill as urgent. SOURCES

The government's own lawyer is now asking for Ressa's acquittal.

In a manifestation and motion dated March 6 and released on Tuesday, March 10, Solicitor General Darlene Berberabe recommended that the Supreme Court overturn the cyber libel conviction of Rappler CEO and Nobel Peace Prize laureate Maria Ressa and former Rappler researcher Reynaldo Santos Jr.

The filing's language was unambiguous.

"Only lawful disposition is acquittal," the OSG stated. THE LEGAL ARGUMENT

The case against Ressa and Santos stems from an investigative article published on Rappler in May 2012. The piece cited intelligence reports linking businessman Wilfredo Keng to alleged criminal activity, including drug trafficking and human trafficking. The same article noted Keng's loan of an SUV to the late Chief Justice Renato Corona.

In February 2014, Rappler updated the article to correct a typographical error. That correction was later treated by the trial court as a "republication," which prosecutors used to argue the Cybercrime Prevention Act of 2012, enacted months after the original publication, could apply retroactively.

Keng filed a complaint with the National Bureau of Investigation in 2017. The NBI forwarded the complaint to the Department of Justice, which filed the cyber libel case in a Manila Regional Trial Court in 2019.

In June 2020, Judge Rainelda Estacio-Montesa convicted Ressa and Santos of cyber libel, sentencing them to imprisonment ranging from six months and one day to six years, and ordering them to pay Keng P400,000 in damages. Rappler as a company was acquitted.

The Court of Appeals upheld the conviction on July 7, 2022. The case was then elevated to the Supreme Court. WHAT CHANGED: THE CAUSING RULING

The pivot point in the OSG's filing is a Supreme Court decision from October 11, 2023: Causing v. People (G.R. No. 258524).

In that case, the Court ruled that cyber libel under Section 4(c)(4) of the Cybercrime Prevention Act is not a new crime. It is the same offense of libel under the Revised Penal Code, committed through a computer system. The penalty is different. The crime is the same.

Because the crime is the same, the prescriptive period for libel under Article 90 of the Revised Penal Code applies. That period is one year.

The trial court and the Court of Appeals had applied Act No. 3326, a 1926 statute governing prescription for special laws, and concluded the prescriptive period for cyber libel was 12 years. The Causing ruling overturned that interpretation.

Berberabe's manifestation accepted the Causing decision and applied it to the Ressa case. The criminal charges were filed in 2019 for an article that, even by the prosecution's own timeline, was last published in 2014. That is five years. One year was the limit.

"Prescription establishes the legal timeframe within which criminal charges must be initiated," the OSG stated. "Once this period expires, the State loses the right to prosecute." THE OSG'S ROLE AND WHAT IT SAID

The Office of the Solicitor General is the government's legal representative in the Supreme Court. In criminal cases, it acts as the "People's Tribune," a role the OSG described in its filing as extending beyond just seeking convictions.

"In the faithful discharge of her duties as an officer of the court and as the People's Tribune, the Solicitor General most respectfully recommends the acquittal of petitioners on the basis of prescription," Berberabe's filing read.

The OSG emphasized that it was not picking sides on the underlying facts of the libel charge. It was acknowledging a legal reality: the Supreme Court's own ruling made prosecution of the case time-barred.

"Upon a considered rethinking of the demand to balance the protection of reputation, privacy, and dignity with the constitutionally guaranteed rights and liberties, the OSG now accepts the Court's judgment in Causing and its consequent application to the instant case." CONTEXT: RESSA AND THE DUTERTE-ERA CASES

The cyber libel conviction was one of 23 legal cases filed against Ressa, Santos, and Rappler since 2018, during the administration of former President Rodrigo Duterte, according to the Hold the Line Coalition. Rappler had published extensive critical coverage of Duterte's drug war and governance.

The tax evasion cases are done. In January 2023, the Court of Tax Appeals acquitted Ressa and Rappler Holdings Corp. in four tax cases. In September 2023, a Regional Trial Court dismissed the fifth and final tax charge. In June 2025, a Filipino court acquitted Ressa and five Rappler directors of violating the Anti-Dummy Law, a charge filed in 2018 alleging foreign ownership violations.

The cyber libel conviction is the last active criminal case against Ressa. If the Supreme Court applies the Causing ruling, she walks free. If it does not, she faces up to six years in prison. WHAT HAPPENS NEXT

The Supreme Court is not bound by the OSG's recommendation. But the government's own legal arm is now telling the Court that its client, the People of the Philippines, has no right to pursue this case.

The International Bar Association's Human Rights Institute was accepted as amicus curiae in the case in April 2024. Three independent expert reports commissioned by the IBAHRI concluded the Cybercrime Prevention Act had been misapplied against Ressa, according to the organization.

The timeline for the Court's decision has not been announced. SOURCES
