[An MCT Commentary]

Sara Duterte cannot stop talking. And every time she does, the country burns a little more.

In the span of a single week — from March 4 to March 10, 2026 — the Vice President has managed to deflect from her own impeachment, attack a whistleblower, offer dangerous foreign policy advice on live television, and stay conveniently silent while her own allies humiliated women in Congress. All while running for president.

Sara Duterte is manufacturing distractions because the walls are closing in — and she knows it. THE IMPEACHMENT SHE CAN'T ESCAPE

On March 4, 2026, the House Committee on Justice voted 54–1 to declare two impeachment complaints against Sara Duterte sufficient in substance. 

The charges are not small: misuse of ₱612.5 million in confidential funds, unexplained wealth, bribery, and threatening to have the sitting President of the Philippines killed.

She has 10 days to respond.

This is the second time the House has moved to impeach her. 

The first attempt in 2025 was killed by the Supreme Court on a technicality — the one-year bar rule. 

That constitutional escape hatch has now expired. There is no more hiding behind procedural loopholes. The case is back, it is stronger, and she knows it.

So what does Sara Duterte do when the evidence piles up? She talks louder about everyone else. "SELECTIVE OUTRAGE" — THE OLDEST TRICK IN THE PLAYBOOK

On the same day the House advanced her impeachment, Sara released a statement calling out the "deafening silence" of civil society groups, religious leaders, and lawmakers over allegations that 18 self-described former Marines delivered suitcases of cash — supposedly billions of pesos — to government officials, including President Marcos himself. She said anger over corruption "should not be selective."

Read that again.

A woman accused of stealing ₱612.5 million in confidential funds is demanding that other people be investigated too. 

She is not denying the charges. She is not presenting evidence of her innocence. She is pointing at other fires and screaming, "Why aren't you looking over there?"

This is deflection so transparent it would be embarrassing if it weren't so effective on her base. 

She praised the 18 ex-Marines not because she cares about truth — she praised them because their claims target the Marcos administration. 

The enemy of her enemy is her headline.

The irony is thick enough to choke on. 

She demands investigations into others while fighting tooth and nail to avoid her own. 

She demands transparency while her confidential fund spending remains buried under contested documents and denials. FILING PERJURY AGAINST THE BAGMAN — ON THE SAME DAY

Also on March 4 — yes, the same day the impeachment complaints were declared sufficient — Sara Duterte personally filed a 340-page perjury complaint against Ramil Madriaga at the Taguig City Prosecutor's Office.

Madriaga is the man who swore in an affidavit that he served as Sara's bagman, claiming he delivered large sums of confidential funds on her orders and that her 2022 campaign was bankrolled by drug money and POGO operators.

Sara says she has never met him. Never spoken to him. Never heard of him until his affidavit surfaced.

The timing of the perjury filing tells you everything. 

She did not file it when Madriaga's affidavit first became public in November 2025. 

She did not file it in December. She did not file it in January or February. 

She filed it on the exact day the House declared her impeachment complaints had substance. 

One attorney for Madriaga asked the only question that needs asking: "Why only now?"

And then there is this detail: Madriaga's legal team claims their client was offered between ₱500 million and ₱1 billion to keep his mouth shut. A fraternity brother allegedly approached him and said, "You don't need to recant, just don't testify."

If Madriaga is lying, a perjury case is warranted. But the timing of the filing — deployed like a press release grenade on the day of an impeachment vote — reeks of damage control, not justice. "JUST SEND AIRPLANES" — FOREIGN POLICY FROM THE BACK SEAT

While the Middle East burns — literally, with the US-Israel war against Iran entering its second week and airspace across the Gulf region closed — Sara Duterte saw an opportunity to play rescuer-in-chief.

Her suggestion: the government should simply dispatch aircraft to bring home distressed overseas Filipino workers.

That's it. Just send planes. Easy. Her father did it during COVID, she said, so what's the problem?

The problem, which any person with a functioning understanding of geopolitics would recognize, is that the airspace is closed. 

It is a combat zone. 

Over 110 flights have been canceled or diverted. Military aircraft in those skies would not be seen as rescue vehicles — they would be targets. 

Sending a C-130 into an active war zone does not save Filipinos. It endangers them.

Palace spokesperson Claire Castro responded plainly: "It is actually impossible to do that at the moment."

President Marcos himself pointed out in a Cabinet meeting that flying into closed Middle Eastern airspace could put Filipinos at greater risk. 

The Department of Migrant Workers has already repatriated 649 OFWs through seven commercial flights since March 5, using open corridors and exit routes that were identified through careful coordination — not reckless grandstanding.

Sara Duterte compared the current crisis to the COVID-19 pandemic, when her father arranged commercial flights for stranded Filipinos. 

That comparison is fucking idiotic. 

A pandemic does not involve anti-aircraft missiles. A virus does not close airspace. 

The logistics of evacuating workers from a country being bombed are fundamentally different from booking a Philippine Airlines charter.

But accuracy was never the point. The point was to look like she cares more than Marcos does. The point was a headline.

After hearing her suggest we fly planes into a closed war zone, the only thing I could think of was: tanga ba 'to? 🤷‍♂️ SILENT ON SUNTAY, SILENT ON WOMEN

While Sara was busy offering unsolicited military advice and crying about selective outrage, one of her own allies — Quezon City Representative Bong Suntay — made a statement during the impeachment hearing and made lewd remarks about actress Anne Curtis to defend her.

Suntay's argument was that thinking about something — like Sara fantasizing about having Marcos killed — cannot be an impeachable offense. 

His analogy: he once saw Anne Curtis at Shangri-La, found her beautiful, and "a desire arose within me." He said he should not be liable for what he imagined.

He said that on the floor of Congress. In a hearing about whether the Vice President threatened the President's life. In front of the country.

The response was immediate. Lawmakers demanded the remarks be stricken from the record. 

Women's groups condemned it. Curtis herself refused to accept Suntay's apology. 

Even Malacañang said the remarks may violate the Safe Spaces Act. 

Ferdinand Topacio, another Duterte ally, made it worse by blaming Curtis for posting "bikini pictures."

And Sara Duterte said absolutely nothing.

Representative Leila de Lima — in a privilege speech during an all-female session celebrating Women's Month — asked the question no one in Sara's camp wants to answer: "Why is the Vice President silent when her fellow women are being degraded by her own allies?"

De Lima's answer: "Is it because those who made these statements are her allies? Is it because their remarks were made in the context of defending her?"

That is the answer. Sara is silent because Suntay was defending her. 

She will not condemn misogyny if misogyny serves her interests. 

She will not stand for women's dignity if standing means losing a political foot soldier.

De Lima also noted that this is the same woman who remains silent on Chinese aggression in the West Philippine Sea, silent while the Chinese embassy disrespects Filipino lawmakers, silent while fishermen are harassed. 

The Duterte family slogan — "Tapang at Malasakit" — is a bumper sticker with no engine behind it. THE PATTERN IS THE POINT

This is who Sara Duterte is. This has always been who she is.

She announced her 2028 presidential bid on February 18, 2026 — days before new impeachment complaints were formally initiated. 

A political science professor from UP called it "an early calculation" designed to send a message to the House: back off, I'm running for president. 

The Makabayan bloc called it a gimmick meant to "strike fear upon congressmen."

She files perjury cases on the same day impeachment advances. 

She invokes her father's legacy every time she needs a shield. 

She talks about corruption only when the accused are her enemies.

She talks about OFWs only when she can embarrass the sitting government. 

She stays quiet when silence protects her allies.

Former Senator Antonio Trillanes, who has been one of the most consistent critics of the Duterte family for years, said it bluntly this week: Sara Duterte is unfit to be president. 

He cited what he called concerns about her mental fitness — the word he used was "krung-krung."

That may be harsh. But look at the record. 

This is a sitting vice president who publicly admitted to arranging the assassination of the President if she died. 

A DepEd secretary who spent ₱612.5 million in confidential funds with no verifiable paper trail. 

A "public servant" who resigned from the Department of Education — the institution responsible for the futures of millions of Filipino children — and walked away like it was a part-time job she got bored of.

And now she wants to be president? 

Tang ina, di ba? 🤷‍♂️ THE FIRE SHE'S REALLY TRYING TO PUT OUT

Every statement Sara Duterte has made this week — every press conference, every perjury filing, every suggestion about airplanes — has one purpose: to keep you from looking at the impeachment case.

The case that says she stole public money. The case that says she lied about her assets. The case that says she threatened to kill the President. The case that now has enough substance — by a vote of 54 to 1 — to move forward.

She is not putting out fires. She is starting new ones so you forget the house is already burning.

Don't forget.

Seventy-one gas stations. Out of eighty-seven inspected. That's the number the Department of Energy came out with on Monday — 71 stations caught violating fuel pricing rules in the middle of a crisis that's already crushing ordinary Filipinos.

Let that ratio sit with you for a second habang nagkakape ka. 

That's not a few bad actors. That's 82% of the ones they bothered to check.

And you know what the DOE did? Issued show-cause orders. Gave them 24 hours to explain themselves.

Pinagpapaliwanag.

As if there's any explanation for jacking diesel up by 54% in one day while jeepney drivers are still figuring out how to get through the week. The Receipts

A gas station in Pasig pushed diesel from ₱51.98 to ₱79.30 per liter. That's not a price adjustment. 

That's robbery with a receipt. 

Over in Fairview, Quezon City, another station moved diesel from ₱48.76 to ₱71 per liter — a 42.68% jump — and when asked, the manager blamed "increased purchase costs." 

A third one in North Fairview hiked from ₱49.75 to ₱72 per liter. The manager refused to comment.

In Echague, Isabela, sixteen stations got notices of violation in a single town. 

Sixteen. 

Mayor Faustino Dy ordered them to stop and explain within 24 hours, or face permit revocation and DOE and DOJ cases. 

In Tagum City, Davao del Norte, one station added ₱8.35 per liter to diesel before any authorized adjustment even kicked in.

And those are just the ones that got caught. There are 14,000 gas stations in this country. The DOE checked 87. Sino ang Totoong Nasasaktan

A jeepney driver burns 15 to 25 liters of diesel a day depending on the route. At a ₱20-per-liter hike, that's ₱300 to ₱500 in extra fuel cost — on top of the boundary fee, which runs ₱800 to ₱1,200 daily.

A driver who works 12 to 14 hours, fights traffic from 4am to sundown, might gross ₱800 to ₱1,200 before expenses. 

After fuel and boundary, some of them are netting less than the guy stacking shelves at a convenience store. 

That's a person who works longer than almost anyone in this country and takes home almost nothing.

And then there's the commuter. 

The factory worker. The construction laborer. The retail employee earning the Metro Manila minimum of ₱695 a day. 

They don't fill up at the pump — but they ride the jeepney, the tricycle, the UV Express. When fuel goes up, fares follow. 

That extra ₱20 or ₱30 per ride, twice a day, six days a week — that's another chunk carved out of ₱695. Before food. Before rent. Before your kid's school supplies.

The gas station owners hiking prices from air-conditioned offices don't know what either of those lives feel like. 

They've never had to choose between a full tank and a full stomach. They've never stood at a terminal at 5am wondering if today's earnings will cover the boundary. Ang Batas na Walang Ngipin

Senator Ping Lacson reminded everyone that premature price hikes violate Republic Act No. 8479, the Downstream Oil Industry Deregulation Act, and RA 7394, the Consumer Act of the Philippines. He told the public to report violations to the PNP and LGUs.

Good. Fine. Report them.

But here's the thing we don't talk about enough. RA 8479 is the same law that took away the government's ability to control fuel prices in the first place. It handed that to "market forces." 

And for 28 years, those market forces have meant one thing: prices go up fast, come down slow, and nobody can do anything because the law says the market decides.

The penalties under RA 8479? For refusing DOE orders — two years imprisonment and a fine of ₱250,000 to ₱500,000. For general violations of the Act — three months to one year and ₱50,000 to ₱300,000. 

The heavier penalties — three to seven years and ₱1 million to ₱2 million — only apply if you can prove cartelization or predatory pricing. Good luck proving that in court when the DOE itself says it doesn't regulate prices.

Energy Secretary Sharon Garin herself said it straight: "The DOE does not regulate prices due to the Oil Deregulation Law. We do not dictate the prices charged to consumers." 

So what exactly is the enforcement mechanism here? Show-cause orders? 24-hour deadlines to explain? Compliance certificates that can be revoked but almost never are?

Pinagpapaliwanag. And then what? Ang Tunay na Krisis

The Philippines imports nearly 90% of its crude oil from the Middle East. Iran closed the Strait of Hormuz, where 20% of the world's oil passes through. 

Brent crude is already approaching $80 per barrel and climbing. 

ING economists flagged the Philippines as among the "worst impacted" countries because our retail fuel prices are market-driven and our subsidies are almost nonexistent.

President Marcos said, "We are victims of a war that is not of our choosing." He's not wrong about that. 

But here's what he didn't say: we are also victims of a deregulated industry that treats a crisis as a business opportunity. 

The war didn't create the profiteering. The war gave profiteers an excuse.

The government ordered a four-day work week for its offices to save fuel. 

It told agencies to cut consumption by 10-20%. 

Meanwhile, Shell is staggering a ₱24.25 per liter diesel hike over three days. 

Petron is rolling out ₱19.20. Caltex, ₱17.50. Seaoil, ₱21.85. These are the legal increases. The ones on top of whatever the 71 violators already pocketed. Ang Magagawa Mo

I'm not going to sugarcoat this. The options are limited. But they're not zero.

- Report. Use the eGovPH app — go to the eReport tab, select "Gas Station Concerns," fill out the details. Screenshots. Timestamps. Receipts. Make them undeniable.

- Report to the PNP and your LGU. Lacson said it, the DOE confirmed it — these are the channels. Don't just post on Facebook. File it where it becomes a record.

- Demand names. The DOE flagged 71 stations but didn't publish a full list with station names and locations. We deserve to know exactly who is profiteering off our desperation. Push for that transparency.

- Push your congressman. RA 8479 needs amendment. Even Malacañang called for it back in 2022. Former Energy Secretary Alfonso Cusi himself said fuel price breakdowns should be public — and that oil companies fought to keep them hidden. Four years later, nothing has changed. Wala Akong Sagot

I don't have a clean ending for this. There's no inspiring takeaway. There's just a number — 71 out of 87 — and a country of 114 million people being told to conserve fuel while gas station owners who got caught cheating are given 24 hours to write an explanation letter.

Pinagpapaliwanag. 

Sana naman, pagkatapos ng paliwanag, may mangyari.

Sana. Sources:

ARAW-ARAW ANG PETSA DE PELIGRO

I've been staring at gas prices all week and I can't get this thought out of my head.

Petsa de peligro. We used to joke about it. A day or two before sweldo, you eat noodles, you stretch what's left, then payday hits and you breathe again. It was a punchline. Something you laugh about with coworkers.

I don't think it's funny anymore.

Because for a lot of Filipinos, petsa de peligro isn't a date. It's Tuesday. It's Thursday. It's the day after payday when you realize the salary already has names on it. Kuryente. Tubig. Utang. Bigas. Pamasahe. And somehow, by the second week, you're back to choosing between buying eggs or cooking oil because both isn't an option.

I need to be honest about something. I'm not living that life anymore. I used to. I remember counting coins for jeepney fare and doing math at the palengke like my life depended on it, because it did. I'm a stay-at-home dad now, a ghostwriter trying to find steady clients, and while things aren't perfect, I'm not the one skipping meals.

But I can't stop thinking about the people who still are. THE PART I CAN'T SHAKE

Half of Filipino families still call themselves poor. Around two out of ten went hungry at least once in the last three months. And I keep hearing the same advice recycled over and over: mag-budget, maghigpit ng sinturon, mag-ipon.

Sinong kinakausap nila?

Someone earning 400, 500 pesos a day in the provinces, stretching that across food, fare, bills, and kids? You want that person to "tighten their belt"? Anong belt? Wala nang butas yung sinturon na 'yun.

Then you open the news and every Tuesday there's a new fuel price hike. Gas up. Diesel up. Kerosene up. Because of the war in the Middle East, they say. Global oil prices. Shipping lanes. Geopolitics.

Fine. I get that part. We import almost everything. When something blows up in the Middle East, we feel it at the pump in Cabanatuan.

But here's what gets me: the Department of Energy is now warning gas stations about hoarding and premature price hikes. They're sending out inspectors. They're coordinating with police. You don't do that if everyone is playing fair.

So there's a war far away driving prices up, AND there are people right here using the chaos to squeeze more out of every liter. Yung gera, negosyo pa rin sa iba.

And who absorbs all of it? The jeepney driver whose 12-hour shift now barely covers diesel. The factory worker choosing between riding or eating. The nanay doing palengke math at 6am, already knowing the numbers won't work. NINAKAW PA YUNG DAPAT SANA TUMUTULONG

This is the part where I feel genuinely angry. Not internet-angry. The kind that sits in your chest.

While ordinary Filipinos are being told to sacrifice, to understand, to adjust, we find out that hundreds of billions in flood control money went to a handful of contractors. Ghost projects. Substandard dikes that collapse when it rains hard. Infrastructure that exists on paper and nowhere else.

Then when typhoons hit and people die, the conversation becomes: "Bakit kasi nagtayo diyan?" "Bakit hindi nag-evacuate?"

Nobody asks: "Bakit kasi ninakaw yung pondo na dapat nandun para protektahan sila?"

We slid further down the global corruption index. Growth forecasts got cut. Investors are pulling back. And inside government, the response is still "we're looking into it" and "these are isolated."

Hindi isolated ang gutom. Hindi isolated ang baha. Hindi isolated ang pamasaheng tumaas na naman. TAPOS SINO YUNG GUSTONG MAMUNO?

And while all this is happening, 2028 is creeping in.

I see names being floated for president, and ang una kong tanong: ano ba ginawa nito? What has this person actually managed? What do they know about fiscal policy, about agriculture, about the price of a kilo of galunggong?

Because our presidential qualifications are basically: be 40, be Filipino, be a voter, be able to read. That's the bar. I've seen harder screening processes for call center applicants.

And the commentary around the race is already about "winnability" and "name recall" and "social media following." Parang talent show audition, hindi pamumuno ng bansa.

So we tell ordinary people: mag-aral ka pa, mag-upskill ka, pagbutihin mo sarili mo. But the people who want to run the country? Sapat na yung apelyido at TikTok account.

I don't know what to do with that. I really don't. Except sit here and feel the weight of it and write it down because keeping it inside feels worse.

Petsa de peligro was supposed to be a joke. A day or two before sweldo.

Hindi na siya nakakatawa.

Three million barrels per day. Gone.

Iraq, the second-largest producer in OPEC, has seen crude output from its main southern oilfields fall from 4.3 million barrels per day to just 1.3 million, according to Reuters, citing three industry sources on Sunday, March 8.

That is a 70 percent collapse in the span of ten days.

The oil fields are intact. The wells work. Iraq has nowhere to send what it pumps. STORAGE IS FULL. EXPORTS HAVE STOPPED.

An official from Iraq's state-run Basra Oil Company told Reuters that crude storage has reached maximum capacity. Whatever production remains is being redirected entirely to domestic refineries.

In February, Iraq exported 3.334 million barrels per day from its southern terminals. By Sunday, that figure had cratered to roughly 800,000 barrels per day, according to Reuters. Only two tankers, the Hong Kong-flagged Cospearl Lake and the China-flagged Yuan Hua Hu, managed to load. Each took on approximately 2 million barrels.

After those ships finished loading around 8 p.m. local time, no new vessels arrived. Three oil officials and a shipping agent confirmed to Reuters that export flows from southern terminals had completely halted.

A senior Iraqi oil ministry official called it "the most significant operational challenge Iraq has encountered in over two decades." THE STRAIT OF HORMUZ CHOKEPOINT

Every barrel of Iraqi crude from the south must pass through the Strait of Hormuz, the waterway between Iran and Oman that carries approximately 20 percent of the world's oil and liquefied natural gas, according to Reuters.

Since the United States and Israel launched military operations against Iran on February 28, the strait has been effectively closed. Iran's Revolutionary Guards warned they would target any vessel attempting passage. Tanker traffic fell from 37 vessels on February 27 to zero, according to Reuters.

Bloomberg reported on March 3 that Iraq had already begun shutting down its Rumaila field, the country's largest, along with the West Qurna 2 project. By March 8, Bloomberg estimated the production drop at roughly 60 percent. Hours later, Reuters confirmed the situation had worsened to 70 percent. UAE, KUWAIT, AND QATAR ARE NEXT

Bloomberg reported on March 8 that the United Arab Emirates and Kuwait have also started reducing output as their own storage fills. Kuwait's petroleum corporation confirmed "precautionary reductions" tied to "Iranian threats against safe passage of vessels through the Strait of Hormuz," according to CNBC.

JPMorgan estimated that actual production cuts across the region reached approximately 2 million barrels per day and warned that cuts could exceed 4 million barrels per day by March 13, according to TradingKey.

Qatar's Energy Minister Saad al-Kaabi told the Financial Times that all Gulf oil-producing nations could cease production entirely within weeks if the blockade persists, potentially pushing prices to $150 per barrel. WHAT THIS MEANS FOR THE PHILIPPINES

According to MUFG Research, 95 percent of the crude oil the Philippines imports comes from the Middle East. BusinessWorld, citing Department of Energy data, places the figure at roughly 98 percent.

Nearly all of the Philippines' imported crude passes through or originates from the zone now under blockade.

Brent crude breached $111 per barrel on Sunday. West Texas Intermediate climbed past $114. U.S. crude surged approximately 35 percent last week, the largest single-week gain in futures trading history since 1983, according to CNBC.

For Filipino consumers, the pipeline from Iraq's collapse to the gas station is direct.

On March 10, Philippine oil companies will begin implementing the largest fuel price increases in the country's history. Diesel is set to rise by P17 to P24 per liter across companies, gasoline by P7 to P13 per liter, and kerosene by P32 to P38.50 per liter, according to the Department of Energy and multiple Philippine outlets.

MUFG Research estimated that every $10 per barrel increase in oil prices widens the Philippines' current account deficit by 0.4 to 0.5 percent of GDP. At $100 per barrel sustained, GDP growth could fall to 3.7 percent in 2026. At $130, it could drop below 3.4 percent.

Around 2.1 million Filipinos live across the Middle East. The Department of Migrant Workers has declared the Strait of Hormuz and the Persian Gulf a "War Risk Operations Area," giving Filipino seafarers the legal right to refuse boarding vessels transiting the zone. THE CLOCK

Iraq relies on oil revenue for over 90 percent of its national income, according to Reuters. With exports halted and production gutted, the fiscal pressure on Baghdad is acute.

Goldman Sachs warned on March 6 that if Strait flows remain sluggish through March, oil prices could surpass their historical peaks from 2008 and 2022.

The wells in Basra are still there. The oil is still underground. The refineries still work. It is the passage out that is gone. SOURCES

🟥 P7 BILLION SHORT: THE REPATRIATION MATH FOR 1,979 OFWS AS THE MIDDLE EAST BURNS

Seven billion pesos.

That is the gap between what the Philippine government has budgeted for OFW repatriation and what it will actually cost if the Middle East conflict forces a full-scale pullout, according to Senator Sherwin Gatchalian.

In an interview on Super Radyo dzBB on Sunday, Gatchalian laid out the arithmetic. The 2026 national budget allocates P6 billion for repatriation. But worst-case estimates, including undocumented Filipino workers, push the actual need to P13 billion, according to GMA News.

The Department of Migrant Workers had already flagged the shortfall, saying it would need a supplemental budget to cover a worst-case scenario. The Overseas Workers Welfare Administration said repatriation costs per individual, initially pegged at P135,000 to P140,000, could reach P150,000 after the escalation, according to GMA News.

🟥 WHO IS ASKING TO COME HOME

As of March 5, 1,979 OFWs across nine countries in the Middle East had formally requested repatriation, according to the Department of Migrant Workers.

Kuwait accounted for the largest number at 633, according to GMA News. The rest were distributed across Bahrain, Abu Dhabi, Dubai, Qatar, Israel, and other posts in the region.

These are the ones who have come forward. The Department of Foreign Affairs counts more than 2.4 million Filipinos residing across the Middle East. Not all of them want to leave. But the number asking is growing.

🟥 THE ONES ALREADY MOVING

Repatriations are happening in waves.

On Saturday, 81 OFWs arrived back in the Philippines. On Sunday, 61 more landed at NAIA Terminal 3 from Dubai aboard Emirates Flight EK 334, including three minors, according to GMA News. They received financial and transportation assistance, temporary accommodation, and psychosocial support on arrival.

Separately, 11 Filipinos were evacuated from Iran to Van, Turkey, through the coordinated efforts of the Philippine Embassies in Tehran and Ankara, according to Inquirer. The evacuation was funded by the DFA's Assistance-to-Nationals Fund. Those 11 are scheduled to fly to the Philippines in the coming days.

An estimated 800 Filipinos remain in Iran.

🟥 ONE WOUNDED, ONE DEAD

One Filipino national in Doha, Qatar, sustained minor injuries from intercepted missile shrapnel, according to the Philippine Embassy in Doha. The individual is safe and recovering, according to GMA News.

The conflict has already claimed one Filipino life. Mary Ann Velasquez de Vera, a caregiver from Pangasinan working in Israel since 2019, died after being struck by shrapnel from an Iranian missile during the February 28 attacks on Tel Aviv. She was accompanying her elderly ward to a bomb shelter when the strike occurred, according to GMA News and the Philippine News Agency.

The Embassy of Israel in the Philippines formally mourned her death and recognized her service.

🟥 THE SEA ROUTES ARE NOW WAR ZONES

On Saturday, DMW Secretary Hans Leo Cacdac signed DMW Advisory No. 11, Series of 2026, classifying the Persian Gulf, the Strait of Hormuz, and the Gulf of Oman as "Warlike Operations Areas," according to Inquirer and ABS-CBN.

Under the advisory, Filipino seafarers can refuse to sail through these waters.

"Filipino crew members, especially ship owners employing Filipino personnel, should avoid operating in that region. If they must go there, they must grant Filipino crew members the autonomy to decline sailing and steer clear of these dangerous waters," Cacdac said in a video message Saturday night, according to Inquirer.

The Philippines supplies a significant share of the global seafarer workforce. The advisory sends a signal well beyond Filipino sailors.

🟥 CONGRESS STEPPING IN

House Majority Leader Ferdinand Alexander "Sandro" Marcos announced Sunday that Speaker Faustino Dy III has requested a joint briefing by the House Committees on Foreign Affairs and Overseas Workers Affairs, set for Wednesday, March 11, according to GMA News and Inquirer.

The briefing will review the conditions faced by OFWs in the conflict zone and the preparedness of government agencies to intervene.

"The House wants to receive a full briefing on the situation on the ground and on the readiness of our agencies to assist our nationals should conditions require government intervention," Marcos said, according to GMA News.

The DFA and DMW are expected to present the number of affected OFWs, security conditions, and contingency plans.

The Senate had already held similar hearings on March 4 and 5, according to Inquirer.

🟥 THE BIGGER PICTURE

The P7 billion gap is a budget problem. But the scale behind it is a human one.

Fuel prices are set to spike further. Minimum estimated increases for next week: P19 per liter on diesel, P9 on gasoline, P31 on kerosene, according to GMA News. If global oil averages above US$80 per barrel for three consecutive months, excise taxes on petroleum are automatically suspended under the TRAIN Law, costing the government an estimated P15 billion per month in lost revenue.

Repatriation costs, the oil shock, and the seafarer advisory are three threads of the same crisis. Together, they compound.

And 1,979 Filipinos are still waiting.

🟥 FIVE OFFICIALS NOW SUING THE 18 EX-MARINES AS CATAPANG FILES AT DOJ AND DE LIMA HITS BACK WITH CYBERLIBEL AND PERJURY CHARGES

The legal walls around the 18 individuals claiming to be former Marines are closing from multiple directions.

On Monday, Bureau of Corrections Director General Gregorio Pio Catapang Jr. filed criminal complaints against the 18 alleged ex-Marines and their lawyer, Atty. Levito Baligod, at the Department of Justice. Catapang is now the fifth official named in the group's allegations to strike back with formal legal action, according to Inquirer and Philstar.

Hours later, House Deputy Minority Leader Leila de Lima of the ML party-list and Fr. Flavie Villanueva filed their own complaints: a cyberlibel case before the National Bureau of Investigation and a perjury charge before the DOJ, both targeting the same 18 individuals and Baligod, according to GMA News.

These are separate actions from separate complainants, all filed on the same day. The legal convergence is notable.

🟥 WHAT CATAPANG SAID

Catapang, who served as Chief of Staff of the Armed Forces of the Philippines before his retirement in 2015, told reporters he felt betrayed by the involvement of his former subordinates.

"Perhaps I encountered some of them while I was still in service, before I retired in 2015," Catapang said, according to Inquirer. He described the decision to pursue legal action against former soldiers as one that brought him sorrow.

"I am compelled to take this step because my name is being implicated," he said.

Despite the legal filings, Catapang left the door open for a resolution. He said he might reconsider if the individuals retracted their statements and acknowledged that the delivery of eight suitcases never took place.

"My conscience is clear," Catapang said, according to Philstar.

He also suggested the 18 might have been misled by external parties, maintaining his long-held belief that "there are no bad soldiers, only bad officers or bad commanding officers."

🟥 WHAT DE LIMA AND VILLANUEVA ARGUED

De Lima and Villanueva denied the allegations against them. The 18 individuals, assisted by Baligod, had accused them of accepting bribe money from former Ako Bicol party-list Representative Zaldy Co as part of what the group described as a "case build-up" against former President Rodrigo Duterte, according to GMA News.

De Lima pointed to what she called the accusers' inconsistency, noting their accounts shifted from describing cash deliveries in suitcases to cash in paper bags. She argued the shifting details exposed the claims as fabrications.

Baligod, for his part, responded: "Ready and willing to face all accusations against us. I hope those mentioned in the Affidavit will also face our accusations," according to GMA News.

🟥 THE BACKGROUND

Baligod presented the 18 individuals in late February as former Marines who allegedly acted as intermediaries delivering an alleged P805 billion in kickbacks tied to fraudulent flood control projects. The group accused multiple officials, including President Ferdinand Marcos Jr., House Speaker Romualdez, and former Representative Zaldy Co.

The Palace dismissed the claims. Press Officer Claire Castro called the allegations a "lousy script" designed to tarnish the President.

The Philippine Navy subsequently said four of the 18 were not affiliated with the service, and a significant number had received dishonorable discharges.

🟥 NBI SUBPOENAS AND SAFE HOUSE OFFER

The NBI had already subpoenaed the 18 individuals on March 5 after the Ombudsman authorized the agency to conduct an inquiry, according to Inquirer and ABS-CBN. NBI Director Melvin Matibag confirmed the subpoenas were issued.

On Sunday evening, the NBI went a step further. The agency announced it was willing to provide a safe house for the 18 alleged ex-Marines, according to Inquirer. The offer followed earlier claims by Baligod that his clients faced security threats after going public with their allegations.

Ombudsman Jesus Crispin Remulla had earlier remarked that the actions of the 18 appeared to be "motivated by political interests," noting that six of the signatories face murder charges.

🟥 WHAT COMES NEXT

Five separate complainants have now filed legal actions against the 18 and Baligod. Earlier, De Lima and Villanueva filed their initial complaint on March 8, and the NBI's own inquiry runs in parallel.

Duterte himself remains detained at the ICC facility in The Hague, where he has been held for a year pending confirmation of charges related to crimes against humanity over thousands of drug war deaths during his presidency and his time as Davao City mayor.

The flood control scandal that spawned these cross-accusations has already led to the detention of former Senator Bong Revilla and former Representative Zaldy Co, with the Sandiganbayan issuing writs of preliminary attachment against multiple properties.

Where the truth sits among the counter-filings will depend on evidence, not volume.

🟥 US ORDERS DIPLOMATS OUT OF SAUDI ARABIA AS OIL SMASHES PAST $111 AND THE IRAN WAR ENTERS WEEK TWO

The United States State Department on Sunday ordered non-essential government employees and their families to leave Saudi Arabia.

It was the first evacuation directive for any American embassy in the Middle East since the war began on February 28. The order cited rising dangers from armed confrontations, terrorism, and missile and drone assaults originating from Yemen and Iran.

That single sentence carries weight.

Washington has maintained diplomatic presence in Saudi Arabia through every regional crisis for decades. Pulling staff now signals something the official statements have been careful to avoid saying outright: the conflict zone is expanding, and the Gulf is no longer a safe perimeter.

🟥 OIL AT LEVELS NOT SEEN SINCE 2022

Brent crude, the international benchmark, surged more than 20 percent on Sunday, briefly reaching $111.04 per barrel, according to Bloomberg. West Texas Intermediate, the US benchmark, climbed past $114 per barrel, up 25 percent from Friday's close of $92.69, according to DW.

These are prices the world has not seen since July 2022.

Since the coordinated US-Israeli strikes on Iran began on February 28, crude has risen approximately 50 percent, according to Al Jazeera. The driver is not just the fighting itself but what the fighting has done to the Strait of Hormuz.

About 20 percent of the world's crude oil and liquefied natural gas passes through that narrow waterway between Iran and Oman. As of last week, according to Reuters, the number of oil tankers transiting the strait had fallen to zero, down from 37 on February 27, the day before the first strikes.

Iran's Revolutionary Guards have warned they will target any vessel attempting to pass through. Cargo ships have ceased operations in the strait entirely.

🟥 THREE OPEC GIANTS CUTTING OUTPUT

Iraq, the United Arab Emirates, and Kuwait, three of OPEC's largest producers, have begun reducing output as storage capacity fills up with nowhere to ship, according to Bloomberg and Al Jazeera.

Qatar's Energy Minister Saad al-Kaabi warned in an interview with the Financial Times that all oil-producing nations in the Gulf could cease production within weeks if the situation persists. He assessed that such a scenario could push oil prices as high as $150 per barrel.

The Philippines imports nearly 90 percent of its oil from the Middle East, according to ING Think. Every dollar added to the barrel price feeds directly into the fuel costs that Filipino consumers pay at the pump.

On March 10, Philippine oil companies will implement staggered diesel price increases of P17 to P24 per liter, the largest single-week fuel hike in the country's history.

🟥 IRAN ATTACKS GULF STATES, SAUDI INTERCEPTS DRONES TARGETING OIL FIELD

Iran continued striking targets across the Gulf on Monday, according to DW.

Saudi Arabia's defense ministry reported it intercepted and destroyed four Iranian drones en route to the Shaybah oil field in eastern Saudi Arabia, near the UAE border. A separate Saudi civil defense report said a projectile strike in Al Kharj province killed two people and wounded 12 others, according to ABC Australia.

In Bahrain, an Iranian drone strike on Sitra island injured 32 people, including children and a two-month-old baby, four of them severely, according to DW. Multiple explosions were reported in Doha, Qatar.

Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian released a video earlier apologizing for strikes against neighboring countries, saying Tehran would cease hostilities against nations not assisting the US and Israel. But Saudi Arabia responded that Iranian attacks were continuing and that the pretexts were baseless, warning of consequences "now and in the future," according to CNBC.

🟥 MOJTABA KHAMENEI NAMED SUPREME LEADER

Iran's Assembly of Experts designated Mojtaba Khamenei, the son of slain Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, as the country's new supreme leader, according to Al Jazeera, BBC, and DW.

The appointment came after the elder Khamenei was killed in the initial US-Israeli strikes on February 28. Israel immediately warned that anyone assuming the position of supreme leader would face assassination threats.

Former CIA Director David Petraeus told CNN the choice was "unfortunate," characterizing Mojtaba as a continuation of his father's hardline ideologies.

US President Donald Trump said on Truth Social that the new supreme leader "will not last long" without American approval, and that no negotiations would occur until Iran agrees to an "unconditional surrender."

🟥 GLOBAL MARKETS IN FREEFALL

The oil shock rippled through Asian markets on Monday.

S&P 500 futures fell 1.7 percent and Nasdaq Composite futures dropped 1.9 percent. Japan's Nikkei 225 futures plummeted 7 percent, according to Al Jazeera.

The Philippines is not insulated. The PSEi fell below 6,000 last week for the first time since 2020, and the peso touched a record low of P59.50 against the dollar. Economists have warned that sustained oil prices above $100 per barrel could push Philippine inflation past the BSP's 2 to 4 percent target range and force an interest rate reversal.

🟥 THE PHILIPPINE EXPOSURE

The Strait of Hormuz closure is not an abstract geopolitical event for the Philippines. It is a direct supply line severed.

An estimated 2.1 million Filipinos live across the Middle East, according to Philippine diplomatic and migration data. About 1.1 million are deployed land-based OFWs. As of early March, 1,979 OFWs from nine countries have requested repatriation, with Kuwait accounting for 633.

The DMW on Sunday declared the Strait of Hormuz and the Persian Gulf a "War Risk Operations Area," giving Filipino seafarers the legal right to refuse boarding vessels transiting through the zone.

And the fuel math is unforgiving. At $111 per barrel, the Philippines is facing a price regime not seen in years. The government is weighing whether to invoke the TRAIN law provision that suspends fuel excise taxes when global prices exceed $80 per barrel. The House Ways and Means Committee held an emergency hearing last week, where the DOF estimated that suspending excise taxes would cost P136 billion in 2026 revenue.

There is no version of this crisis that does not reach every Filipino household.

Vice President Sara Duterte said the government should just send planes.

Palace Press Officer Undersecretary Claire Castro, speaking to reporters in New York City while accompanying President Ferdinand Marcos Jr. on a United Nations working visit, said Duterte does not know what she is talking about.

"First and foremost, this reflects the issue with those who do not engage in study, do not contemplate, and are unaware of the government's actual programs," Castro said on Sunday, March 9, responding to Duterte's latest public criticism of the administration's handling of the Middle East crisis. WHAT SARA DUTERTE SAID

In a recent interview, Duterte described the government's repatriation efforts as inadequate and said it was "too easy to send planes" to the Middle East to bring Filipinos home.

She cited friends who had managed to return from Dubai even as commercial airspace across parts of the Middle East remained restricted due to the ongoing US-Israel military campaign against Iran.

Duterte also claimed that a large number of overseas Filipino workers chose not to return because there were no jobs waiting for them under the Marcos administration. CASTRO'S FULL RESPONSE

Castro did not hold back.

"It is certainly preferable for our leaders to possess the ability to think critically and make decisions that benefit not just themselves but the entire populace," she said.

She drew a line between government decisions informed by intelligence, interagency coordination, and on-the-ground assessments versus decisions based on what one friend experienced.

"If decisions are made solely on such anecdotes, the entire nation could potentially face challenges if we rely only on friends or rumors for our decision-making," Castro said.

The Palace press officer emphasized that the government's repatriation program operates under specific protocols established by the Department of Migrant Workers, the Department of Foreign Affairs, and the Philippine Overseas Labor Office. These protocols account for airspace restrictions, airline availability, diplomatic coordination with host countries, and the safety of Filipinos being moved through active conflict zones.

Castro's core argument was that the Vice President was basing her criticisms on isolated personal accounts rather than the full operational picture. THE REPATRIATION IN NUMBERS

As of the latest DMW figures, close to 2,000 Filipino workers have requested repatriation from Middle East countries affected by the Iran conflict. Over 2.1 million Filipinos are estimated to be working across the region.

The DMW last week expanded its declaration of a war risk zone to cover the entire Persian Gulf, triggering additional benefits and protections for affected workers.

Commercial flights remain limited across parts of the Gulf, with Iran, Iraq, and portions of the Arabian Peninsula subject to airspace closures or restrictions. The Philippine government has been coordinating chartered flights and military airlift options, though logistics remain constrained.

Duterte's suggestion that planes could simply be sent does not account for the airspace restrictions, the diplomatic clearances required, or the fact that many OFWs are spread across dozens of employers and locations in countries that are themselves under threat. THE POLITICAL CONTEXT

The exchange is the latest in a long series of public clashes between the Marcos administration and the Vice President.

Duterte survived an impeachment vote in the House of Representatives earlier this month by a narrow 54-1 margin on substance. She remains the subject of multiple investigations and legal proceedings, including ICC-related cases tied to her father's drug war.

Castro's remarks in New York carry a specific subtext: the Palace is willing to publicly confront the Vice President on policy substance, not just political posturing. 🟥 SUSPENDING FUEL EXCISE TAX WOULD COST THE PHILIPPINES P136 BILLION IN 2026, DOF TELLS HOUSE HEARING

The Department of Finance put the number on the table on Monday afternoon: P136 billion.

That is how much the Philippine government stands to lose in revenue if it suspends the excise tax on all petroleum products from May to December 2026, according to Finance Undersecretary Karlo Fermin Adriano.

Adriano delivered the estimate during a hearing of the House committee on ways and means, chaired by Rep. Stella Quimbo, which convened to examine proposals to temporarily eliminate or reduce fuel excise taxes as the Middle East war pushes oil prices past $100 per barrel.

Of the P136 billion, P121.4 billion would come from lost excise tax collections. The remaining P14.6 billion would be from reduced value-added tax (VAT) revenues tied to lower fuel prices.

When Quimbo asked whether that figure covered a full 12-month period, Adriano clarified it did not. The P136 billion applies only to the eight months from May through December.

🟥 THE PER-PESO MATH

Adriano broke it down further.

Every peso removed from the current P6 per liter excise tax on diesel would cost the treasury P9 billion to P10 billion in lost revenue.

Every peso removed from the P10 per liter excise tax on gasoline would cost P5 billion to P6 billion.

These are not abstract numbers. Congress is actively weighing proposals from multiple legislators to grant President Ferdinand Marcos Jr. the authority to suspend or reduce fuel excise taxes. The President himself has said he may seek emergency powers from Congress to do exactly that.

Energy Secretary Sharon Garin said on Sunday the government was aiming to secure the excise tax suspension before Congress goes on recess.

🟥 TWO SCENARIOS FROM NEDA

The National Economic and Development Authority presented two scenarios at the same hearing.

Scenario one: the Middle East conflict ends soon and oil prices settle around $78 per barrel. Under that scenario, diesel would drop from a projected P74.22 per liter in March to about P67.50 in April. With the excise tax suspended, it would fall further to around P60.61.

Scenario two: the Strait of Hormuz closes and oil spikes to $140 per barrel. Diesel would surge to P96 per liter. Suspending the excise tax under that scenario would bring it down to around P90, according to NEDA Undersecretary Rosemarie Edillon.

For gasoline, the worst-case scenario projects prices reaching P82.20 per liter. Removing the full P10 excise tax would bring it down to about P72.

Inflation projections diverge sharply between the two scenarios. The optimistic case yields inflation around 4.51 percent from March to June. The worst case pushes it to 6.0 to 6.5 percent, well above the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas target range of 3 to 4 percent.

🟥 THE LEGISLATIVE LANDSCAPE

Multiple proposals are circulating in both chambers of Congress.

Rep. Quimbo has introduced a bill to amend Section 148 of the National Internal Revenue Code, which would give the President standing authority to adjust excise taxes on petroleum products without needing emergency powers each time.

In the Senate, Joel Villanueva filed Senate Bill 1922, authorizing the President to suspend or reduce excise taxes once global crude oil prices reach or exceed $80 per barrel based on the Mean of Platts Singapore benchmark. Senate Majority Leader Juan Miguel Zubiri has expressed support. Sen. Francis Pangilinan called for both excise tax suspension and the activation of fuel subsidy programs for transport workers, farmers, and fisherfolk.

Sen. Panfilo Lacson noted that a similar automatic suspension mechanism existed under the TRAIN Law but expired in 2020, and suggested Congress either grant emergency powers or amend the law to restore it.

🟥 THE CLOCK IS RUNNING

The hearing comes one day before the largest single fuel price increase in Philippine history takes effect, with diesel set to rise by up to P24.25 per liter starting Tuesday.

Congress is scheduled to recess soon. If the excise tax suspension does not pass before legislators leave, Filipino consumers will absorb the full price surge with no government buffer for weeks.

The P136 billion question is whether Congress considers that cost acceptable against what happens if it does nothing.

🟥 PSEI CRASHES BELOW 6,000 FOR FIRST TIME SINCE 2020 AS PESO FALLS TO ALL-TIME LOW P59.50 AGAINST THE DOLLAR

The Philippine Stock Exchange index closed at 5,970.64 on Monday, March 9, according to Yahoo Finance data. That is a single-day plunge of more than 5 percent from the previous session, and the first time the PSEi has breached the 6,000 psychological floor since the early months of the COVID-19 pandemic.

Investagrams pegged the close at 6,006.22, a decline of 4.97 percent, still hovering just above that critical 6,000 level. TradingEconomics reported 5,992. Regardless of which number you use, all three confirm the same reality: the Philippine stock market just had its worst single-day performance in years.

The intraday low was 5,965.03.

🟥 THE PESO SIMULTANEOUSLY COLLAPSED

At the same time, the Philippine peso closed at P59.50 against the US dollar on Monday, a new all-time low, according to ABS-CBN News and the Manila Bulletin.

The Bankers Association of the Philippines reported an intraday low of P59.71, meaning the peso briefly traded at its weakest level in history during the trading session. The high for the day was P59.25, still deep in record territory.

TradingEconomics recorded the USD/PHP exchange rate at 59.635 on March 9, up 0.83 percent from the prior session. Over the past month, the peso has weakened 1.93 percent.

This was the peso's eighth record low in 2026 alone.

🟥 WHAT IS DRIVING BOTH CRASHES

The two-front financial collapse has a single cause: the ongoing US-Israel military campaign against Iran.

With oil above $100 per barrel and the Strait of Hormuz under threat, the Philippines is uniquely exposed. The country imports roughly 100 percent of its crude oil needs. Every dollar increase in oil prices translates directly into inflation, higher transport costs, and weaker corporate earnings.

Foreign investors have been pulling out of the Philippine stock market for weeks. Reuters reported Monday that the Iran conflict is forcing central banks across Asia into a "sharp policy rethink," with emerging economies like the Philippines caught between defending their currencies and supporting economic growth.

IMF Managing Director Kristalina Georgieva warned at a Tokyo symposium on Monday that a sustained 10 percent increase in oil prices could add 40 basis points to global inflation.

"My counsel to policymakers in this evolving global context is to consider the unimaginable and prepare for it," Georgieva said, according to Reuters.

🟥 CONTEXT: THE MARKET WAS ALREADY FALLING

The PSEi closed at 6,320.41 on March 6. It had been at 6,134.62 on March 4. The decline has been accelerating.

From its recent levels above 6,300, the index has now shed more than 300 points in a single session. Investagrams notes that the 6,000 level is a key technical support. If it breaks convincingly, the next area traders are watching is in the 5,800 range.

The peso, meanwhile, was already approaching 59 per dollar before Monday. It closed at 59.00 on March 6. Now it has blown past that number.

🟥 THE BSP DILEMMA

The Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas (BSP) faces a painful choice.

If it raises interest rates to defend the peso, it risks choking off economic growth at a time when businesses are already reeling from fuel costs. If it holds steady, the peso could slide further, making imports more expensive and feeding inflation.

BSP Governor Eli Remolona has already signaled openness to a rate increase. Meralco has warned that higher power rates may appear on April bills. The DOE projects diesel prices could exceed P80 per liter this month.

Everything is moving in the same direction. And Monday's market numbers suggest investors believe it will get worse before it gets better.

🟥 SHELL LEADS WITH P24.25 DIESEL HIKE AS OIL COMPANIES ANNOUNCE STAGGERED PRICE INCREASES STARTING MARCH 10

Here is what Filipino motorists are waking up to on Tuesday.

Shell Pilipinas will raise diesel prices by P14.55 per liter on March 10, followed by P7.30 on Wednesday and P2.40 on Thursday. That is P24.25 per liter in total across three days, the steepest increase among all oil companies this week, according to the Department of Energy.

Petron follows at P19.20. The first blow lands on Tuesday at P11.60 per liter, then P3.80 daily until the total is reached.

Total stations will hike diesel by P20.20 per liter, split into two equal phases of P10.10.

Seaoil is set to increase by P21 to P23 per liter, also in two phases.

Jetti Petroleum will add P19 per liter. Tuesday brings only P4, followed by P3 per liter for the next five days.

Chevron offers the smallest total increase at P17.50 per liter, spread across seven daily increments of P2.50 each.

🟥 HOW THE STAGGERED APPROACH CAME ABOUT

Energy Secretary Sharon Garin announced the staggered schedule during a briefing at the House committee on ways and means hearing on Monday, March 9.

"The price now is not the highest in history, but the one coming, that will be implemented tomorrow, will be the highest jump," Garin told lawmakers.

Garin clarified that the DOE has no authority to regulate prices because the oil industry is deregulated under Republic Act 8479. Oil companies report their planned adjustments to the DOE, not the other way around.

"What I can give you that is positive about the news today is that they have agreed to stagger it in the sense that in the whole week, hindi isang bagsakan na P20 agad tomorrow," she said.

The variation across companies is wide. Some will front-load the increase with a P10 or P14 per liter jump on Tuesday. Others, like Chevron and Jetti, will drip it out over five to seven days at P2.50 to P4 per liter daily.

🟥 THE FULL PICTURE: GASOLINE AND KEROSENE

Diesel is not the only fuel surging.

Gasoline prices will rise by P7 to P13 per liter across all retailers starting Tuesday. The DOE-Oil Industry Management Bureau had projected a minimum of P9 per liter for gasoline.

Kerosene, which affects the poorest households that still rely on it for cooking and lighting, faces the steepest proportional increase: P32 to P38.50 per liter.

This marks the ninth consecutive week of gasoline increases and the eleventh for both diesel and kerosene. The DOE had set suggested price ceilings for March 6 to 9, including diesel at P49 to P66.59 per liter and regular gasoline at P49 to P64.70 per liter. After Tuesday, those ranges become obsolete.

🟥 DOE PRICE CEILING RANGES (MARCH 6-9)

Gasoline RON 97/100: P53.70 to P76.50 per liter.

Gasoline RON 95: P50.00 to P71.04 per liter.

Gasoline RON 91: P49.00 to P64.70 per liter.

Diesel: P49.00 to P66.59 per liter.

Diesel Plus: P56.80 to P74.81 per liter.

Kerosene: P78.90 to P99.89 per liter.

These ceilings are expected to be revised sharply upward following Tuesday's implementation.

🟥 THE MIDDLE EAST FACTOR

The entire fuel crisis traces back to February 28, when the United States and Israel launched Operation Epic Fury against Iran. Twelve days later, the war continues. Oil prices have exceeded $100 per barrel for the first time since 2022, and the Strait of Hormuz, through which roughly a fifth of global oil supply passes, remains under threat.

The DOE earlier warned that diesel could exceed P80 per liter this month if the Middle East war drags on and price increases are not staggered.

For the average Filipino jeepney driver, tricycle operator, or delivery rider, the math is brutal. A diesel vehicle that consumes 20 liters daily now faces an additional P350 to P485 in fuel costs per day, or P2,450 to P3,395 per week.

🟥 NBI ISSUES SUBPOENAS TO 18 ALLEGED BAGMEN OF ZALDY CO, SAYS UP TO 38 MAY TESTIFY IN FLOOD CONTROL KICKBACK PROBE

Thirty-eight people. That is how many individuals the National Bureau of Investigation says may come forward as alleged bagmen of former Ako Bicol party-list Representative Elizaldy "Zaldy" Co.

NBI Director Melvin Matibag disclosed the figure during an ambush interview on Monday, March 9, explaining that out of 103 individuals believed to have been recruited by Co, 38 consented to cooperate.

Eighteen have already come forward. Matibag said roughly 20 more are expected to follow.

The NBI issued subpoenas to the initial 18 individuals on Friday, March 6, according to the Philippine Daily Inquirer.

🟥 NOT ALL MARINES

The 103 individuals were not exclusively former military. Matibag clarified that the group included drivers and ordinary civilians alongside former Marines.

Attorney Levito Baligod, who first presented the 18 alleged bagmen during a February 24 press conference in San Juan, confirmed on his Facebook page that Co had 103 security personnel with what he described as "gated access."

The Philippine Navy, however, has challenged the identities of those who came forward. According to the Navy, four of the 18 were never associated with the Philippine Marines. The rest, the Navy said, had been dishonorably discharged.

Ombudsman Jesus Crispin Remulla went further. In a statement reported by ABS-CBN News on March 5, Remulla said the 18 supposed ex-Marines were making public statements to incite other soldiers to rebel against the government.

The Ombudsman also noted that six of the signatories face murder charges, according to the Inquirer.

🟥 THE ALLEGATION: P805 BILLION IN KICKBACKS

The original claim is staggering in scale.

During their February 24 press conference, the 18 individuals alleged they had personally delivered approximately P805 billion in suspected kickbacks from irregular flood control projects to government officials.

Named recipients, according to the affidavits submitted by Baligod to the Office of the Ombudsman on February 26, allegedly include former House Speaker Martin Romualdez, President Ferdinand Marcos Jr., and Co himself.

The affidavit identified seven senators and 26 members of the House of Representatives as recipients.

Baligod later admitted what he called an "oversight" regarding the inclusion of Mamamayang Liberal party-list Representative Leila de Lima in the list, clarifying that her situation involved a paper bag rather than a suitcase.

🟥 PALACE AND NBI PUSH BACK

Palace Press Officer Claire Castro dismissed the allegations as a "poorly written script" and labeled them a smear campaign against the President.

The NBI itself has not taken the claims at face value. Matibag expressed concern about an effort to "influence perceptions of the audience" and said the agency is examining all possibilities and leads.

Former congressman Mike Defensor, who acknowledged providing assistance to the group, was also summoned by the NBI alongside Baligod. Defensor denied involvement in creating their affidavits. The attorney who notarized the affidavits was summoned as well.

All of them were scheduled to appear at the NBI on March 11, according to the Philstar.

Matibag said the NBI was authorized by the Ombudsman to conduct the investigation. He also encouraged Baligod to voluntarily provide affidavits from each of the former Marines for individual interviews, with subpoenas to be issued if necessary.

🟥 FLOOD CONTROL CONTEXT

The bagmen allegations sit within a much larger scandal.

Under the Marcos administration, the government funded 9,855 flood control projects worth over P545 billion from July 2022 to May 2025, according to the Philippine Center for Investigative Journalism. Senate Blue Ribbon Committee Vice Chairperson Erwin Tulfo described the program as "a grand robbery," claiming kickbacks and commissions as high as 25 percent left only 30 to 40 percent of funds for actual construction.

Co's construction firm, Sunwest Corporation, is at the center of one case already before the courts. The Sandiganbayan Fifth Division recently ordered the seizure of 16 properties belonging to Co and his wife Mylene, valued at P215 million, in connection with a P289 million flood control project in Naujan, Oriental Mindoro, that government inspectors found to be substandard.

Co left the Philippines on August 6, 2025, approximately three months before the Ombudsman filed criminal charges against him in November. The Sandiganbayan has declared him a fugitive from justice.

🟥 WHAT COMES NEXT

The NBI probe is ongoing.

Whether any of the 38 potential witnesses produce evidence that corroborates their claims remains to be seen. For now, the subpoenas are on the table, the Ombudsman's office is watching, and the investigation is following the evidence.

Thirty-eight potential witnesses. A P805 billion allegation. A fugitive ex-congressman. And a flood control program that cost the Philippines over half a trillion pesos in three years.

🟥 DMW EXPANDS WAR RISK ZONE TO ENTIRE PERSIAN GULF — 2.1 MILLION FILIPINOS IN MIDDLE EAST, NEARLY 2,000 SEEKING REPATRIATION

How fast is the danger zone expanding?

On Saturday, March 8, the Department of Migrant Workers classified the Strait of Hormuz as a war risk area. By Sunday, March 9, Secretary Hans Cacdac had extended that classification to cover the entire Persian Gulf.

Two days. Two declarations. The second one dwarfs the first.

The Strait of Hormuz is a narrow chokepoint between Iran and Oman. The Persian Gulf is the body of water that connects Iran, Iraq, Kuwait, Saudi Arabia, Bahrain, Qatar, and the UAE. Declaring the whole Gulf a "War Risk Operations Area" means Filipino seafarers assigned to vessels operating anywhere in those waters now have the legal right to refuse the assignment without contract penalties.

🟥 THE REPATRIATION COUNT

As of March 9, a total of 1,979 Filipinos across nine Middle East countries have formally requested repatriation, according to the DMW. Kuwait leads the count at 633.

Eleven Filipinos were safely evacuated from Iran to Van, Turkey, with coordination between the Philippine embassies in Tehran and Ankara. An estimated 800 Filipinos remain in Iran who may wish to return.

On Sunday, 61 OFWs — including three minors — landed at NAIA Terminal 3 aboard Emirates Flight EK 334 from Dubai. The day before, 81 OFWs had returned from the Middle East through separate flights.

One Filipino in Doha, Qatar, was wounded by shrapnel from an intercepted missile. The injuries were reported as minor, and the individual is recovering.

🟥 2.1 MILLION IN THE REGION

The DMW's own 2025 data puts the total number of Filipinos living across the Middle East at approximately 2.1 million. Of those, roughly 1.1 million are deployed land-based OFWs under formal labor contracts.

That means the 1,979 requesting repatriation so far represent less than 0.1 percent of the total Filipino population in the region. The gap between the number who want to leave and the number who are there is enormous — and it could shift quickly depending on how the conflict evolves.

🟥 CONGRESS STEPS IN

House Speaker Faustino Dy III has ordered a joint briefing by the Committee on Foreign Affairs and the Committee on Overseas Workers Affairs, scheduled for Wednesday, March 11. The briefing is expected to cover the scope of the repatriation effort, the safety of Filipino workers and seafarers in the conflict zone, and the government's capacity to handle a larger-scale evacuation if needed.

🟥 THE FUEL CONNECTION

The Persian Gulf classification is not just a labor issue. It is directly tied to energy supply.

The Strait of Hormuz, now classified as a war risk zone since March 8, handles roughly 20 to 21 percent of daily global oil consumption. Iran has threatened to block all vessel traffic through the strait. Qatar has already declared force majeure on its liquefied natural gas operations. Iraq has reduced oil output due to the Hormuz blockage.

The Philippines imports over 90 percent of its fuel. The Department of Energy has signaled that fuel prices will see double-digit increases starting March 10. Goldman Sachs has raised its Brent crude forecast by $10 per barrel.

When the DMW designates the Persian Gulf a war risk zone, it is responding to a military conflict that is simultaneously a shipping crisis and an energy crisis. Filipino seafarers are caught in the middle of all three.

🟥 WHAT THE DESIGNATION MEANS FOR SEAFARERS

The "War Risk Operations Area" classification comes from the Warlike Operations Area Committee of the International Bargaining Forum. Under this framework, Filipino crew members on vessels flagged under any country — Panama, Liberia, Marshall Islands, or otherwise — gain specific protections: the right to refuse assignment, enhanced insurance coverage, and additional compensation for war-risk sailing.

Shipowners employing Filipino crew are required to inform their personnel of the designation before any transit through the affected zones. Crew members who decline cannot be penalized or have their contracts terminated.

The Philippines supplies an estimated 25 percent of the global merchant shipping workforce. A significant portion of those seafarers serve on tankers and carriers that routinely pass through the Persian Gulf.

🟥 SANDIGANBAYAN ORDERS SEIZURE OF 16 PROPERTIES OWNED BY FORMER CONGRESSMAN ZALDY CO IN P289 MILLION FLOOD CONTROL GRAFT CASE

The anti-graft court is now moving against Zaldy Co's assets.

The Sandiganbayan Fifth Division has issued a writ of preliminary attachment against 16 real estate properties belonging to former Ako Bicol party-list Representative Elizaldy "Zaldy" Co and his wife, Mylene, in connection with the allegedly substandard P289 million flood control project in Naujan, Oriental Mindoro.

The order allows the court sheriff to attach enough of Co's property in the Philippines to satisfy a potential civil liability amounting to P215 million, should Co be convicted.

🟥 THE LEGAL BASIS

The court granted the government prosecutors' ex-parte motion, citing Rule 127 of the Rules of Court on Provisional Remedies in Criminal Cases.

The Sandiganbayan said the rule applies when a criminal action involves money or property "embezzled, fraudulently misapplied, or converted" by a public officer or any person in a fiduciary capacity.

"The present case squarely falls within the ambit of Section 2(b), Rule 127, as it is predicated on a claim involving public funds allegedly embezzled, fraudulently misapplied, or converted by the accused Department of Public Works and Highways officers in conspiracy with the accused Co," the court stated.

The Sandiganbayan further cited what it described as fraud "inherent in a violation of Section 3(e) of RA No. 3019, the Anti-Graft and Corrupt Practices Act, when evident bad faith is alleged, as in the present case."

The prosecution confirmed in its Affidavit of Merit that there is no other sufficient security for the claim.

"Unless his properties are immediately attached, the State may be unable to satisfy any future judgment, as accused Co may easily alienate or conceal his remaining assets in the Philippines," the court said.

🟥 THE FLOOD CONTROL PROJECT

The case traces back to a P289.5 million road dike along the Mag-Asawang Tubig River in Naujan, Oriental Mindoro. The project was supposed to be a flood control measure. What government inspectors found when they examined it was something else.

On September 9, 2025, DPWH Secretary Vince Dizon and Oriental Mindoro Governor Bonz Dolor personally inspected the project site. According to the findings reported by the Philstar and confirmed by Dizon's own testimony at the Sandiganbayan, the steel sheet piles used in the dike were "significantly below the required standards."

The Commission on Audit's Region 4B auditor, Victoriano Acosta, testified during bail hearings that COA issued a notice of disallowance worth P84 million out of the P289 million budget after determining the project was "constructed not in accordance with plans and specifications," according to GMA News.

🟥 CO AS A FUGITIVE

Co left the Philippines on August 6, 2025, approximately three months before the Office of the Ombudsman filed the first criminal charges against him at the Sandiganbayan on November 18, 2025.

The Sandiganbayan has declared Co a fugitive from justice. On March 5, the court denied his motion for reconsideration of that status, calling it both filed beyond the reglementary period and lacking in merit, according to a GMA News report.

Co is one of 16 individuals facing charges. The others include officials from the DPWH-Mimaropa regional office and board members of Sun Corporation, a construction firm owned by Co.

The charges filed by Assistant Ombudsman Aserico Clavano include malversation of public funds through falsification of documents under the Revised Penal Code and violation of Section 3(e) of the Anti-Graft and Corrupt Practices Act. For Co specifically, the Ombudsman also filed charges under Section 3(b) of the same law, concerning claims of receiving undue financial or material advantages.

🟥 THE WIDER FLOOD CONTROL RECKONING

This case is one piece of a much larger wave. The flood control corruption scandals that erupted in 2025 brought hundreds of thousands of Filipinos into the streets, triggered the resignation of former House Speaker Martin Romualdez, and led to a nationwide audit of flood control contracts.

Co's case was the first flood control-related criminal case filed at the Sandiganbayan, according to the Ombudsman. Other cases remain under preliminary investigation.

DPWH Secretary Dizon has stated publicly that the evidence against Co is "robust" and "unequivocal." During an impromptu interview at the Sandiganbayan in January, Dizon said: "The evidence will clearly demonstrate that the project was subpar, that former Rep. Zaldy Co and officials from the DPWH colluded to execute a deficient project to misappropriate funds meant for our citizens."

Co's counsel, lawyer Ruy Rondain, has not yet publicly responded to the writ of preliminary attachment, according to GMA News.

🟥 MOJTABA KHAMENEI IS NOW SUPREME LEADER OF IRAN AND HIS FIRST ACT WAS TO ORDER MISSILES FIRED AT ISRAEL AND THE GULF

The succession is no longer a question.

Shortly after midnight on March 9, Iranian state broadcaster IRIB confirmed what had been expected for days: Mojtaba Khamenei, the 56-year-old son of the late Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, has been named Iran's new supreme leader. The Assembly of Experts, Iran's 88-member clerical body responsible for selecting the top leader, made the decision.

Within hours, Iran's president, the Revolutionary Guards, and the armed forces pledged full allegiance to Mojtaba Khamenei, according to Al Jazeera.

Then came the missiles.

🟥 THE FIRST STRIKES UNDER THE NEW LEADER

IRIB posted a photo on Telegram showing a projectile with the words "At Your Command, Sayyid Mojtaba." The caption read: "Iran fires first wave of missiles under the leadership of Ayatollah Seyyed Mojtaba Khamenei toward occupied territories."

Missiles and drones were launched not only toward Israel but across the Gulf. Saudi Arabia, Qatar, Kuwait, Bahrain, the United Arab Emirates, and Iraq all reported incoming projectiles overnight, according to Radio Free Europe/Radio Liberty.

In Bahrain, an Iranian drone attack wounded 32 civilians, four of them seriously, including children, according to Bahrain's Health Ministry. Witnesses later reported thick smoke rising from the direction of Bahrain's Bapco oil refinery, which had already been damaged by Iranian missiles four days earlier, according to Reuters.

Explosions rocked the Qatari capital, Doha. The Saudi Foreign Ministry issued a statement warning that continued Iranian attacks would have "serious impact" on relations "now and in the future," condemning what it called "blatant Iranian attacks against a number of Arab countries and the resulting targeting of civilians, vital facilities, and diplomatic missions."

Israel said one woman was moderately injured in the missile barrage before the all-clear was given.

Hours before the new leader's appointment, the Israeli military announced it had carried out a strike on a hotel room in Beirut's Raouche district, killing several senior commanders of the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps' Quds Force.

According to sources cited by Saudi-owned Al Hadath and reported by Iran International, five Iranian members of the Quds Force's Lebanon branch were killed, including the branch's financial officer, intelligence officer, and senior operatives handling Lebanon and Palestine operations.

Lebanon's state-run National News Agency reported four fatalities and ten injuries. The Israeli military said the commanders were "operating to advance terror attacks against the state of Israel and its civilians."

🟥 THE SCHOOL, THE CHILDREN, THE INVESTIGATION

One of the gravest reported incidents on Day 10 involved an elementary school in Iran.

Iranian state media reported that a strike on a school killed at least 168 children. CNN reported that the incident is now under US investigation, with video footage suggesting that a US airstrike targeting a nearby naval base may have caused the casualties. No official confirmation or denial has been issued by the US military as of this writing.

This figure remains unverified by independent organizations. As required by MCT's reporting standards, the source of this casualty claim is Iranian state media.

According to Iran's health ministry, cited by Al Jazeera, more than 1,300 people have been killed in Iran since the war began on February 28.

The broader toll, as compiled from multiple wire services and government statements: 1,332 dead in Iran, 339 in Lebanon, 11 in Israel, 7 US military personnel, 3 in the UAE, 2 in Iraq, 2 in Saudi Arabia, and 1 across Oman and Bahrain, according to figures attributed to the respective governments and media outlets.

Oil prices eclipsed $115 per barrel on Monday, according to the Associated Press. That is the highest level since the 2022 surge following Russia's invasion of Ukraine.

For the Philippines, which imports nearly 90 percent of its oil from the Middle East, every dollar added to the barrel price translates directly to the pump. Diesel at most Philippine stations is set to rise by P17 to P24 per liter starting Tuesday, March 10, in staggered hikes confirmed by Energy Secretary Sharon Garin.

The Strait of Hormuz, through which roughly 20 percent of the world's oil transits, remains effectively shut to commercial shipping. Reuters reported that tanker crossings dropped to zero by midweek. Lloyd's List reported traffic down 81 percent, with just one crude oil tanker crossing on March 1 and no LNG carriers at all. War risk insurance for the region expired on March 5, and no new tankers have been willing to enter the Middle East Gulf, according to Lloyd's List.

President Donald Trump called the oil price surge a "small price to pay" to eliminate Iran's nuclear threat. The US State Department ordered nonessential diplomatic personnel and families at the embassy in Riyadh to leave Saudi Arabia, according to the New York Times.
